E2B03.explanation: The correct answer is "By scanning odd-numbered lines in one field and even-numbered lines in the next" because that is the definition of interlaced scanning. The two fields combine into one full frame.
E2B03.mnemonic: Interlace = odd field, then even field.
why_wrong: Interlace does use two fields, but not by scanning them simultaneously.
The fields are not created by reversing vertical scan direction.
Horizontal scan direction does not alternate left-to-right then right-to-left between fields.

E2B04.hint: Analog SSTV usually sends the color components one after another instead of putting them all on a TV-style subcarrier at once. Think sequential color lines.
E2B04.explanation: The correct answer is "Color lines are sent sequentially" because many analog SSTV systems send separate color information in sequence for each image line. This is different from broadcast-TV style chroma schemes such as color bursts or subcarriers.
E2B04.mnemonic: Analog SSTV color comes one line component at a time.
why_wrong: A 2.8 kHz subcarrier description sounds more like a broadcast-TV idea than the SSTV method tested here.
Color-burst wording belongs to conventional color TV, not this analog SSTV question.
This is not how analog SSTV color encoding is being described in the pool.

E2B05.hint: Vestigial sideband keeps most of one sideband and a small vestige of the other. That saves bandwidth while still preserving low-frequency video detail better than full sideband removal would.
E2B05.explanation: The correct answer is "Vestigial sideband reduces the bandwidth while increasing the fidelity of low frequency video components" because VSB is a compromise between full AM and full single-sideband filtering. It narrows the required channel while keeping enough of the opposite sideband to preserve important low-frequency video information.
E2B05.mnemonic: VSB trims bandwidth but leaves a vestige for video quality.
why_wrong: The vestigial sideband is not there mainly to carry the audio information.
It is also not a special chroma-only sideband.
High-frequency picture emphasis is not the reason VSB is used.

E2B06.hint: Vestigial sideband is basically AM with one full sideband plus a leftover piece of the other sideband. That leftover piece is the vestige.
E2B06.explanation: The correct answer is "Amplitude modulation in which one complete sideband and a portion of the other are transmitted" because that is the textbook definition of vestigial sideband. It is not inverted-sideband AM, narrowband FM, or a spread-spectrum method.
E2B06.mnemonic: VSB = one whole sideband plus a vestige of the other.
why_wrong: VSB does not mean one sideband is inverted.
This describes an FM idea, not vestigial-sideband AM.
Spread spectrum has nothing to do with the definition of VSB.

E2B07.hint: DVB-T is a digital television system, so expect digital phase and amplitude constellations rather than analog FM or AM. The right pair is QAM and QPSK.
E2B07.explanation: The correct answer is "QAM and QPSK" because DVB-T uses digital modulation methods based on phase and amplitude constellations. FM/FSK and AM/OOK are not the standard DVB-T answer in this context.
E2B07.mnemonic: DVB-T rides on QAM and QPSK.
why_wrong: FM and FSK are not the digital-TV modulation pair used for DVB-T here.
AM and OOK do not describe the DVB-T modulation pair either.
Because the other modulation pairs are wrong, 'all these choices' cannot be correct.

E2B08.hint: To use ordinary analog TV receivers on 70 centimeters, amateurs transmit on frequencies that line up with cable-TV channel assignments those receivers can tune. Think shared cable-TV channels.
E2B08.explanation: The correct answer is "Transmitting on channels shared with cable TV" because commercial analog TV receivers can tune those channelized frequencies even though they are being used in the amateur 70-centimeter context. The other answers do not explain receiver compatibility.
E2B08.mnemonic: Commercial analog TV receiver trick: use cable-channel frequencies.
why_wrong: Satellite dishes are unrelated to making a standard analog TV tune amateur fast-scan TV on 70 centimeters.
Old broadcast channel numbers are not the compatibility trick used here.
A USB sound-card setup is not how ordinary analog TV receivers are made compatible with this operation.

E2B09.hint: DRM-based SSTV decoding is usually done from an SSB receiver so the software can hear the signal in the proper passband. Think sound-card digital mode workflow with an SSB rig.
E2B09.explanation: The correct answer is "SSB" because DRM and similar digital image modes are typically received through a communications receiver or transceiver running in SSB mode, feeding software that performs the decoding. CDMA and AREDN are unrelated technologies, and plain AM is not the normal answer here.
E2B09.mnemonic: Digital image decoding usually starts with an SSB receiver.
why_wrong: CDMA is a multiple-access cellular-style technique, not the receiver type asked about.
AREDN is a mesh-network platform, not an SSTV receiver class.
AM is not the normal receiver mode used for this DRM-based SSTV decoding setup.

E2B10.hint: In analog SSTV, picture brightness is mapped to audio pitch. Brighter or darker parts of the picture correspond to different tone frequencies.
E2B10.explanation: The correct answer is "Tone frequency" because SSTV brightness information is encoded by the frequency of the transmitted audio tone. It is not carried by tone amplitude, and the sync signals are for timing rather than picture brightness.
E2B10.mnemonic: SSTV brightness rides on pitch, not loudness.
why_wrong: Amplitude would mean loudness, but SSTV picture brightness is not encoded that way.
Sync amplitude is about timing structure, not brightness information.
Sync frequency also relates to timing, not image brightness.

E2B11.hint: The VIS code tells the receiving software which SSTV format to expect. Think mode identification so the decoder knows how to interpret the picture timing.
E2B11.explanation: The correct answer is "To identify the SSTV mode being used" because the VIS code lets the receiver determine which SSTV standard or format is being transmitted. It is not for call sign ID, vertical sync itself, or color-burst locking.
E2B11.mnemonic: VIS = tell the receiver which SSTV mode this is.
why_wrong: Color-burst locking belongs to conventional color-TV ideas, not the VIS function.
VIS is not the vertical-sync signal itself; it identifies the SSTV mode.
Station identification is a different function from VIS mode signaling.

E2B12.hint: SSTV software watches for the timing tones that mark line starts. So the trigger for a new picture line is specific tone frequencies.
E2B12.explanation: The correct answer is "Specific tone frequencies" because SSTV uses defined audio-frequency markers for synchronization and line timing. The software is not simply waiting a fixed elapsed time, looking for amplitude changes, or waiting for just any two-tone burst.
E2B12.mnemonic: New SSTV line starts when the right sync tones arrive.
why_wrong: Elapsed time alone is not reliable enough; the decoder looks for the designated timing tones.
The trigger is based on frequency markers, not on tone amplitude.
Just hearing two tones is not enough; the decoder is looking for the designated line-sync frequencies.

E2C01.hint: If the remote transmitter is still in the US, you do not need to tack on a state, district, or section suffix. The normal call sign is enough.
E2C01.explanation: The correct answer is "No additional indicator is required" because US-licensed operators controlling a US-based remote transmitter still identify normally. The rule does not require a state abbreviation, an /R district tag, or an ARRL section suffix for this domestic remote-control situation.
E2C01.mnemonic: US remote in the US means normal ID only.
why_wrong: There is no rule requiring a state abbreviation after the call sign in this situation.
An /R district indicator is also not required by the rules here.
ARRL section identifiers are contesting or organizational concepts, not required ID suffixes.

E2C02.hint: For exchanging amateur log data between logging programs and services, the common standard is ADIF. This is one of the basic ham-software acronym questions.
E2C02.explanation: The correct answer is "ADIF" because the Amateur Data Interchange Format is the standard file format used to exchange logging information. NEC is for antenna modeling, and the other distractors are not standard ham log-exchange formats.
E2C02.mnemonic: ADIF = Amateur Data Interchange Format for logs.
why_wrong: NEC is associated with antenna modeling, not contact-log exchange.
ARLD is not the standard file format used for ham log interchange.
OCF is associated with antenna terminology, not logging data exchange.

E2C03.hint: Thirty meters is the band that is generally kept out of contesting. It is a narrow, shared band with special operating restrictions, so it is the standard contest-exclusion answer.
E2C03.explanation: The correct answer is "30 meters" because amateur contesting is generally excluded from that band. The other listed bands are commonly used in contests, but 30 meters is treated differently due to its limited allocation and operating rules.
why_wrong: 6 meters is heavily used in VHF contesting, so it is not the excluded band here.
70 centimeters is also a common contest band and not the general exclusion.
33 centimeters is not the standard answer to the no-contesting question.

E2C04.hint: Amateur mesh networks are commonly built on bands shared with Wi-Fi-like unlicensed data services. Think Part 15-style hardware adapted for amateur use.
E2C04.explanation: The correct answer is "Frequencies shared with various unlicensed wireless data services" because amateur mesh networking commonly uses hardware and spectrum near existing unlicensed wireless data bands. HF digital segments, cable TV channels, and 60-meter channels are not the normal mesh-network answer here.
E2C04.mnemonic: Ham mesh often rides where unlicensed data gear already lives.
why_wrong: HF digital frequencies are not the usual amateur mesh-network platform.
Cable TV channels are not the normal frequencies used for amateur mesh networking.
60 meters is not where amateur mesh networks are commonly implemented.

E2C05.hint: A DX QSL manager handles the paperwork side of confirmations for a station, especially one that is hard to reach directly. Think cards and confirmations, not frequencies or pileup control.
E2C05.explanation: The correct answer is "Handle the receiving and sending of confirmations for a DX station" because a QSL manager's job is to process QSL cards or confirmation requests on behalf of the DX station. That is administrative follow-up, not frequency allocation, net control, or propagation advising.
why_wrong: Frequency planning is not what a QSL manager does.
Running a net is a different operating role, not QSL management.
Advising a DXpedition about openings or pileups is not the main QSL-manager task.

E2C06.hint: During VHF/UHF contests, SSB and CW activity gathers in the weak-signal part of the band, especially around the calling frequency area. That is where serious weak-signal operators already live.
E2C06.explanation: The correct answer is "In the weak signal segment of the band, with most of the activity near the calling frequency" because VHF/UHF contest operators using SSB and CW normally congregate in the weak-signal portions of the bands. They do not move to some special contest-only segment at the top or sit on the middle FM calling frequencies.
E2C06.mnemonic: VHF/UHF contest SSB/CW stays in the weak-signal segment.
why_wrong: There usually is not a special top-of-band contest-only segment for this activity.
The middle of the band is often where FM activity lives, not the main SSB/CW contest concentration.
This also points to the wrong part of the band and the wrong operating culture.

E2C07.hint: Cabrillo is the common contest-log submission format. Think contest sponsors and electronic log files, not on-air exchanges.
E2C07.explanation: The correct answer is "A standard for submission of electronic contest logs" because Cabrillo defines how contest logs are structured for submission and scoring. It is not the over-the-air exchange itself, not a rulebook, and not a radio protocol.
why_wrong: The contest exchange is what operators send on the air, not the Cabrillo file.
Contest rules may mention Cabrillo, but Cabrillo itself is not the ruleset.
Cabrillo is a file format, not a digital on-air transmission protocol.

E2C08.hint: LoTW can confirm a lot more than just ordinary DX contacts. If several normal confirmation uses are listed, this is one of those real all-of-the-above questions.
E2C08.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because Logbook of The World can be used to confirm special-event contacts, international contacts, and award-credit contacts such as Worked All States. The system supports all of those confirmation categories.
E2C08.mnemonic: LoTW covers many kinds of valid confirmations, so all three fit.
why_wrong: This is true, but it is only one valid use of LoTW.
This is also true, but not the only correct choice.
This one is true as well, which is why the combined answer wins.

E2C09.hint: Modern amateur mesh networks are commonly built from Wi-Fi-style routers flashed with ham-oriented firmware. Think modified wireless router, not packet TNCs or EchoLink.
E2C09.explanation: The correct answer is "A wireless router running custom firmware" because that is the common hardware platform for amateur mesh networking such as AREDN-style systems. Traditional packet-radio modems and EchoLink setups are different technologies.
E2C09.mnemonic: Ham mesh box = wireless router with custom firmware.
why_wrong: A 1200-baud VHF packet setup is classic packet radio, not modern mesh networking hardware.
EchoLink connects radio to the internet, but it is not the typical amateur mesh-network implementation.
A 9600-baud UHF packet setup is still packet radio, not the common mesh platform asked here.

E2C10.hint: Split operation helps a DX station manage a pileup, keep callers separate, and deal with band-plan or licensing differences. This is another question where all three listed reasons are real.
E2C10.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because DX stations often work split to separate their transmit frequency from the pileup, reduce interference and improve efficiency, and sometimes operate in a way that accommodates responding stations with different permitted frequencies. All three reasons can apply.
E2C10.mnemonic: DX split solves several problems at once, so all three reasons fit.
why_wrong: This can be true, but it is only one reason DX stations use split.
This is also a valid reason, but not the whole answer by itself.
This is true too, which is why the combined answer is correct.

E2C11.hint: In a DX pileup or contest, the efficient and correct practice is to send your full call sign once or twice, cleanly. More words usually make things worse, not better.
E2C11.explanation: The correct answer is "Send your full call sign once or twice" because that gives the DX station enough information without adding unnecessary clutter. Sending only part of your call is poor practice unless the DX station specifically asks for it, and adding extra text or your grid usually just slows the pileup down.
E2C11.mnemonic: Pileup etiquette: full call, once or twice, then listen.
why_wrong: Sending only the last two letters is not proper unless the DX station has already narrowed it down to that partial call.
A grid square is usually unnecessary in a DX pileup unless the exchange specifically requires it.
Long 'DX station this is...' style calls just add clutter and reduce efficiency.

E2C12.hint: If there is a delay between pressing a control and hearing or seeing the result on the transmitted signal, that delay is latency.
E2C12.explanation: The correct answer is "Latency" because latency is the time delay between an action and its observable result in the system. Jitter is variation in timing, hang time is a specific repeater/VOX-style delay behavior, and anti-VOX is a control feature rather than the delay itself.
why_wrong: Jitter is variation in delay, not the basic delay itself.
Hang time is a specific hold-open delay concept, not the general action-to-result delay.
Anti-VOX is a control feature, not the name of the delay.

E2D01.hint: MSK144 is the digital mode built specifically for meteor scatter work. If the question says meteor scatter, think MSK144 first.
E2D01.explanation: The correct answer is "MSK144" because that mode was designed to pull information out of the short bursts and Doppler-spread conditions common in meteor scatter communication. WSPR is for propagation study, APRS is for packet position/data reporting, and Hellschreiber is a different keyboard/text mode family.
E2D01.mnemonic: Meteor scatter mode to remember: MSK144.
why_wrong: WSPR is a weak-signal propagation beacon mode, not the standard meteor-scatter mode.
Hellschreiber is not the purpose-built meteor-scatter mode asked here.
APRS is for packet data and tracking, not meteor-scatter QSOs.

E2D02.hint: In VHF FT8/FT4 contesting, location matters more than classic signal reports. The exchange uses your grid square instead of an SNR report.
E2D02.explanation: The correct answer is "Grid square" because VHF contest exchanges on FT8 or FT4 typically use Maidenhead grid locators as the key exchange information. RST, state, or serial-number exchanges belong to other contest formats.
E2D02.mnemonic: VHF digital contest exchange = grid square.
why_wrong: RST is a classic signal-report exchange, but not the replacement used here.
State abbreviations are used in some contests, but not as the FT8/FT4 VHF replacement for SNR.
Serial numbers belong to many contests, but not this specific weak-signal digital exchange.

E2D03.hint: Q65 is the modern weak-signal mode designed with EME in mind. If the question says moonbounce, Q65 should stand out.
E2D03.explanation: The correct answer is "Q65" because Q65 was specifically developed for challenging weak-signal paths such as EME. MSK144 is tied to meteor scatter, WSPR to propagation reporting, and PACTOR III to file/message transfer.
why_wrong: MSK144 is the meteor-scatter specialist, not the EME specialist.
PACTOR III is aimed at robust data transfer, not moonbounce weak-signal work.
WSPR is for propagation measurement, not typical EME QSOs.

E2D04.hint: Real-time balloon tracking in amateur radio is the classic APRS job. If it is position tracking, think APRS before the other modes.
E2D04.explanation: The correct answer is "APRS" because APRS is designed for transmitting position and status information in real time, making it ideal for balloon tracking. FT8 and PACTOR III serve different purposes, and the fake LORAN distractor is not an amateur-balloon tracking system.
why_wrong: FT8 is for weak-signal contacts, not live position tracking.
This is a made-up or unrelated navigation-style term, not the ham-radio tracking method.
PACTOR III is a data-transfer mode, not the standard real-time balloon-tracking tool.

E2D05.hint: JT65 became famous because it could decode extremely weak signals. The big thing to remember is very low signal-to-noise performance.
E2D05.explanation: The correct answer is "Decodes signals with a very low signal-to-noise ratio" because JT65 was built for weak-signal work where ordinary modes would fail. The '65' in the name does not mean 65 Hz bandwidth or 65 baud, and the mode has nothing to do with fast-scan TV.
why_wrong: JT65 is narrow, but the answer is not that it uses exactly 65 Hz bandwidth.
The name does not mean a 65-baud symbol rate.
JT65 is a weak-signal digital mode, not an ATV mode.

E2D06.hint: EME contacts are often made by strict timed sequencing: one station transmits during one period, then the other transmits during the next. Think alternating synchronized periods.
E2D06.explanation: The correct answer is "Time-synchronous transmissions alternating between stations" because moonbounce paths are weak and delayed, so EME operation often relies on carefully timed transmit/receive periods that alternate between the two stations. The other answers do not describe the standard EME contact method.
E2D06.mnemonic: EME works by synchronized alternating sequences.
why_wrong: Store-and-forward is a messaging technique, not a standard way to establish EME contacts.
Moon-reflected beacons may be interesting, but they are not the normal contact-establishment method in this question.
Fast identification is not what defines how EME contacts are arranged.

E2D07.hint: APRS is built on the AX.25 packet protocol. If the question asks APRS protocol, AX.25 is the standard answer.
E2D07.explanation: The correct answer is "AX.25" because APRS uses AX.25 packet framing and addressing conventions in amateur packet-radio operation. PACTOR and AMTOR are other digital protocols, while QAM is a modulation concept rather than the APRS packet protocol.
why_wrong: PACTOR is a different HF digital-data protocol, not the APRS packet protocol.
QAM is a modulation family, not the APRS link protocol.
AMTOR is another digital communications system, not the APRS protocol foundation.

E2D08.hint: APRS beacons are usually sent as UI frames, which are connectionless packet frames. Think beacon broadcast, not connected-session packet traffic.
E2D08.explanation: The correct answer is "Unnumbered Information" because APRS beacon data is generally transmitted in AX.25 UI frames that do not require setting up a connection first. Acknowledgement and connect frames belong to other packet-session behaviors.
why_wrong: An acknowledgement frame is part of confirmed packet exchanges, not the normal APRS beacon format.
Burst is not the AX.25 frame type being asked here.
Connect frames are used for connected packet sessions, while APRS beacons are usually connectionless UI frames.

E2D09.hint: JT65 uses multiple audio tones instead of simple two-tone FSK or PSK. The exam phrase to remember is multitone AFSK.
E2D09.explanation: The correct answer is "Multitone AFSK" because JT65 sends information using multiple audio-frequency tones through an audio-frequency-shift-keying structure. It is not PSK, ordinary RTTY, or QAM.
E2D09.mnemonic: JT65 = many tones, so multitone AFSK.
why_wrong: JT65 is not a phase-shift-keying mode.
RTTY is a different two-tone text mode and not the JT65 modulation method.
QAM is not the modulation used by JT65.

E2D10.hint: In APRS path notation, `WIDE3-1` means the packet started with a request for up to three wide-area hops and now one of those hops remains. The first number is the requested width, and the second tracks what is left.
E2D10.explanation: The correct answer is "Three digipeater hops are requested with one remaining" because APRS path aliases count down as packets are relayed. `WIDE3-1` indicates a path that originally allowed three hops and is now down to one remaining hop.
E2D10.mnemonic: APRS path `3-1` means started at 3, now 1 hop left.
why_wrong: This has nothing to do with how many stations may transmit on a frequency.
Subcarriers are unrelated to APRS path notation.
Internet gateways are not what `WIDE3-1` is counting.

E2D11.hint: APRS stations relay data through packet digipeaters. Think packet repeats and path hopping, not voice repeaters or digital voice systems.
E2D11.explanation: The correct answer is "By packet digipeaters" because APRS is a packet-radio system that relies on digipeaters to repeat and extend data coverage. C4FM and DMR repeaters belong to digital voice ecosystems, and ACK/NAK relay is not the normal APRS relay method.
E2D11.mnemonic: APRS gets around by packet digipeaters.
why_wrong: ACK/NAK signaling is not the main APRS relay architecture being asked here.
C4FM repeaters are for digital voice systems, not standard APRS relaying.
DMR repeaters are also a different system from APRS packet digipeating.

E2E01.hint: For data emissions below 30 MHz, the classic answer is FSK. On HF, think frequency-shift keying rather than phone-style DTMF on FM or spread-spectrum methods.
E2E01.explanation: The correct answer is "FSK" because HF data operation below 30 MHz has traditionally used frequency-shift keying methods. DTMF on FM and spread spectrum do not fit the typical below-30-MHz data-emission rule context in this question, and pulse modulation is not the standard answer being tested.
E2E01.mnemonic: HF data below 30 MHz: think FSK.
why_wrong: DTMF on FM is not the standard below-30-MHz data-emission answer here.
Pulse modulation is not the usual HF data-emission method being tested.
Spread spectrum is not the expected answer for below-30-MHz data emissions in this question.

E2E02.hint: WSJT-X modes such as FT8 depend on very accurate time slots, so the computers on both ends need synchronized clocks. That timing sync is what keeps transmit and receive windows lined up.
E2E02.explanation: The correct answer is "Synchronization of computer clocks" because WSJT-X modes use tightly timed transmission cycles. If the computer clock is off, the software will transmit or listen in the wrong slot. The answer is not frequency alignment or some separate sync-field signal; it is clock synchronization.
E2E02.mnemonic: WSJT-X works when the clocks agree.
why_wrong: Frequency alignment matters for tuning, but it is not what synchronizes the TX/RX timing slots.
WSJT-X does not depend on a separate sync-field transmission for timing in this way.
There is no special sync-pulse mechanism here; the practical answer is synchronized computer clocks.

E2E03.hint: The `4` in FT4 is about the four tones used in the modulation scheme. The direct expansion to remember is four-tone continuous-phase frequency shift keying.
E2E03.explanation: The correct answer is "Four-tone continuous-phase frequency shift keying" because FT4 is named for its four-tone modulation structure. The distractors try to make the `4` mean message-bit grouping or the number of cycles per minute, but the tested meaning is the modulation method itself.
why_wrong: The `4` is not referring to four-bit user-information blocks here.
It is also not naming four transmit/receive cycles per minute.
Because those other meanings are wrong, 'all these choices' cannot be correct.

E2E04.hint: FST4 is a family with several operating options, and the exam is checking whether you recognize that several listed traits are all true at once. This is a real all-of-the-above item.
E2E04.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because FST4 uses four-tone Gaussian FSK, offers variable transmit/receive periods, and includes multiple tone spacings. Each listed statement is a real characteristic of the mode.
E2E04.mnemonic: FST4 has several built-in options, so all three traits fit.
why_wrong: This statement is true, but it is only one part of the full answer.
This is also true, but not the only correct statement.
This one is true too, which is why the combined answer wins.

E2E05.hint: WSPR is a beacon-style propagation-reporting mode, not a keyboard chat mode. If the question asks which mode does not support keyboard-to-keyboard, WSPR should stand out.
E2E05.explanation: The correct answer is "WSPR" because WSPR sends structured weak-signal propagation information rather than live conversational text. RTTY, PSK31, and MFSK16 are all capable of keyboard-to-keyboard style operation.
E2E05.mnemonic: WSPR whispers reports, not live typing.
why_wrong: RTTY is a classic keyboard-to-keyboard text mode.
PSK31 is also well known for keyboard chat.
MFSK16 supports conversational keyboard operation too.

E2E06.hint: FT8 runs on fixed 15-second sequences. That timing is one of the most basic FT8 facts to memorize.
E2E06.explanation: The correct answer is "15 seconds" because FT8 uses a strict 15-second transmission cycle. It does not vary with message length, and it is not 8 or 30 seconds in the standard mode.
why_wrong: FT8 timing is fixed, not variable with message size.
8 seconds is too short for the standard FT8 cycle.
30 seconds is longer than the standard FT8 cycle.

E2E07.hint: Q65 improves weak-signal performance by averaging information over multiple receive periods. That multi-cycle averaging is the key difference the pool wants you to remember.
E2E07.explanation: The correct answer is "Multiple receive cycles are averaged" because Q65 is designed to dig signals out of very difficult conditions by combining information from more than one receive interval. It is not a keyboard-chat mode and it does not use quadrature modulation just because the name starts with Q.
E2E07.mnemonic: Q65 gets stronger by averaging multiple receive cycles.
why_wrong: Q65 is not defined by keyboard-to-keyboard operation.
The `Q` in Q65 does not mean quadrature modulation here.
Because the first two statements are wrong, 'all these choices' cannot be correct.

E2E08.hint: If the question is about transferring binary files over HF, think PACTOR. It is the robust HF data-transfer mode in this answer set.
E2E08.explanation: The correct answer is "PACTOR" because PACTOR is designed for reliable data transfer, including binary file transfer, over HF. PSK31 and RTTY are more text-oriented, and AMTOR is older and not the intended answer here.
why_wrong: PSK31 is mainly a low-bandwidth text-chat mode, not the standard binary-file mode here.
RTTY is also mainly a text mode and not the intended binary-file-transfer answer.
AMTOR is an older digital mode and not the best match for this question.

E2E09.hint: PSK31 uses Varicode, which is a variable-length character code. That makes PSK31 the clear answer when the question mentions variable-length coding on HF.
E2E09.explanation: The correct answer is "PSK31" because PSK31 famously uses Varicode, a variable-length character coding scheme that makes common characters shorter. RTTY uses fixed-length coding, and the other listed modes are not the canonical Varicode answer.
why_wrong: RTTY uses a fixed-length coding scheme rather than variable-length characters.
PACTOR is not the famous variable-length-character mode asked here.
MT63 is not the standard Varicode-based answer to this question.

E2E10.hint: Among the listed modes, FT8 is the narrowest. It is designed to squeeze effective weak-signal communication into very little bandwidth.
E2E10.explanation: The correct answer is "FT8" because FT8 occupies a very narrow slice of spectrum compared with modes like MFSK16, classic RTTY, or PACTOR IV. This narrow bandwidth is part of why so many FT8 signals fit into a small passband.
why_wrong: MFSK16 is wider than FT8.
Classic 170 Hz shift, 45-baud RTTY is also wider than FT8.
PACTOR IV is much wider and optimized for throughput, not minimum bandwidth.

E2E11.hint: Direct FSK changes the transmitter's frequency source directly, instead of making tones in audio first. So the key distinction is that it modulates the transmitter VFO.
E2E11.explanation: The correct answer is "Direct FSK modulates the transmitter VFO" because direct FSK shifts the RF source itself, while audio FSK creates audio tones that later modulate the transmitter. The question is asking for the defining difference, not a guaranteed bandwidth or baud-rate advantage.
E2E11.mnemonic: Direct FSK acts on the VFO directly.
why_wrong: Direct FSK is not defined by always occupying less bandwidth.
It is also not defined by an automatic higher-baud-rate capability.
Because those extra claims are not generally true, 'all these choices' is wrong.

E2E12.hint: ALE works by scanning a programmed list of frequencies and listening for its own call. When it hears the right address, the radio wakes up and links on that channel.
E2E12.explanation: The correct answer is "ALE constantly scans a list of frequencies, activating the radio when the designated call sign is received" because Automatic Link Establishment is all about unattended scanning and selective calling across several candidate channels. It does not depend on internet paging, constant tone transmission, or backscatter echoes.
E2E12.mnemonic: ALE scans channels until it hears its own call.
why_wrong: ALE is not based on watching an internet site for a page frequency.
It also does not establish links by sending one constant tone code.
Backscatter echoes are unrelated to how ALE stations find each other.

E2E13.hint: If the channel is clear and you want the highest throughput among these HF modes, PACTOR IV is the speed king in this answer set.
E2E13.explanation: The correct answer is "PACTOR IV" because it is designed for much higher data throughput than narrow weak-signal or keyboard-chat modes like FT8, MFSK16, or 45-baud RTTY when conditions are good. The question specifically says clear communication conditions, which favors the highest-performance data-transfer mode.
E2E13.mnemonic: Best clear-channel HF throughput here: PACTOR IV.
why_wrong: MFSK16 is slower and more modest in throughput than PACTOR IV.
Classic 45-baud RTTY is much slower than modern PACTOR IV.
FT8 is optimized for weak-signal contacts, not maximum data throughput.

E3A01.hint: EME can work between stations nearly on opposite sides of the Earth as long as both can see the moon. The exam number to remember is about 12,000 miles along Earth's surface.
E3A01.explanation: The correct answer is "12,000 miles, if the moon is “visible” by both stations" because moonbounce depends on simultaneous moon visibility, not on whether the moon is at perigee or apogee. When geometry lines up, two stations can be separated by roughly half the Earth's circumference and still use the moon as the reflector.
E3A01.mnemonic: EME range is almost half the world: about 12,000 miles.
why_wrong: Perigee helps path loss, but it does not shrink the maximum possible separation to only 2,000 miles.
Apogee also does not define the maximum geometry in the way this answer suggests.
5,000 miles is still far below the approximate maximum path separation asked here.

E3A02.hint: Libration fading on EME sounds fluttery and unstable because the moon's surface and motion create many slightly shifting reflections. Think rough flutter, not a smooth fade.
E3A02.explanation: The correct answer is "A fluttery, irregular fading" because libration changes the relative phases of the many reflected signal components coming from the moon. The result is a rough, watery flutter rather than a slow pitch change or steady fadeout.
why_wrong: A slow pitch change suggests Doppler, not the characteristic sound of libration fading.
A gradual loss is too smooth and does not match the irregular fluttering effect.
A lower-frequency returning echo describes Doppler shift, not libration fading.

E3A03.hint: For EME, less distance to the moon means less path loss. So the best case is when the moon is at perigee, its closest point to Earth.
E3A03.explanation: The correct answer is "When the Moon is at perigee" because a closer moon means a shorter two-way path and lower free-space path loss. Full moon vs new moon is mostly visual, not the key radio-path factor here.
E3A03.mnemonic: Perigee = nearer moon = lower EME path loss.
why_wrong: Moon phase does not matter nearly as much as moon distance for this question.
Apogee is the farthest moon position, so it gives more path loss, not less.
MUF is an ionospheric concept and is unrelated to moonbounce path loss.

E3A04.hint: In an electromagnetic wave, the electric field, magnetic field, and direction of travel are all perpendicular to each other. So the wave travels at right angles to both fields.
E3A04.explanation: The correct answer is "It travels at a right angle to the electric and magnetic fields" because electromagnetic propagation is transverse. The fields are not pointing along the travel direction; they are perpendicular to it and to each other.
E3A04.mnemonic: E, H, and travel direction form a three-way right-angle set.
why_wrong: Phase angle does not determine propagation direction in the way this answer suggests.
The wave does not travel parallel to the fields; that would contradict transverse-wave geometry.
The electric-field phase angle also does not set the travel direction.

E3A05.hint: The electric and magnetic parts of an electromagnetic wave are perpendicular to each other. This is a basic wave-geometry fact worth memorizing directly.
E3A05.explanation: The correct answer is "They are at right angles" because the electric and magnetic fields in an electromagnetic wave are orthogonal. They are not parallel, merely tangential, or defined as 90 degrees out of phase in time here.
E3A05.mnemonic: E field and H field cross at right angles.
why_wrong: Parallel fields would not match the geometry of an electromagnetic wave.
Tangential is too vague and not the specific relationship tested here.
The question is about spatial orientation, not a time-phase offset.

E3A06.hint: If the MUF drops after dark, the remedy is to move lower in frequency so the ionosphere can still refract your signal. Think: when MUF falls, go lower.
E3A06.explanation: The correct answer is "Switch to a lower frequency HF band" because a path stops supporting higher frequencies first as the MUF declines. Going to a lower band restores the chance of ionospheric refraction for that path.
E3A06.mnemonic: MUF falling means move down in frequency.
why_wrong: A higher band will fail even sooner if the MUF is already falling.
Antenna takeoff angle is not the main fix when the ionosphere will no longer support the present frequency.
Beam width also does not solve a basic MUF problem.

E3A07.hint: Tropospheric ducts often form where layers of air with different temperature and moisture properties sit over large water surfaces. Think marine ducts over oceans or large lakes.
E3A07.explanation: The correct answer is "Large bodies of water" because water surfaces often support the temperature inversions and stable layers that create tropospheric ducting, especially for microwave signals. Mountains and cloud categories are not the classic answer here.
E3A07.mnemonic: Microwave ducts love large water surfaces.
why_wrong: Mountains can help other propagation effects, but they are not the typical ducting feature tested here.
Stratocumulus clouds are not the standard geographic feature associated with duct formation.
Nimbus clouds are weather features, not the typical answer for microwave-duct geography.

E3A08.hint: Meteor trails produce ionization in the E region, which is why meteor scatter is tied to that part of the ionosphere.
E3A08.explanation: The correct answer is "The E region" because meteor-induced ionized trails form at altitudes associated with the E region. That temporary ionization is what makes meteor-scatter contacts possible.
E3A08.mnemonic: Meteor scatter lives in the E region.
why_wrong: The F1 region is too high for the classic meteor-trail answer.
The F2 region is also too high and is not where meteor trails are usually placed in the exam.
The D region is too low and mostly associated with absorption.

E3A09.hint: Meteor scatter is most practical in the VHF and upper-HF range where ionized trails reflect well but the frequencies are not too high. The pool answer is 28 to 148 MHz.
E3A09.explanation: The correct answer is "28 MHz - 148 MHz" because meteor scatter is commonly used on 10 meters, 6 meters, and 2 meters. Lower HF is too low for the classic sweet spot, while much higher UHF is less effective.
E3A09.mnemonic: Meteor scatter sweet spot: roughly 10 meters through 2 meters.
why_wrong: 160 meters is far too low for the usual meteor-scatter operating range.
10 to 14 MHz is also below the classic meteor-scatter sweet spot asked here.
220 to 450 MHz is generally too high for the best meteor-scatter performance.

E3A10.hint: Wave speed in a medium depends on that medium's refractive properties. The technical term the exam wants is index of refraction.
E3A10.explanation: The correct answer is "The index of refraction" because electromagnetic waves travel slower or faster in different media according to the medium's refractive index. Resistance, reactance, evanescence, and birefringence are not the controlling answer here.
E3A10.mnemonic: Wave speed through a medium follows its refractive index.
why_wrong: Resistance and reactance are circuit concepts, not the direct answer for wave speed in a medium.
Evanescence describes a field behavior, not the main speed-setting property asked here.
Birefringence can affect polarization behavior, but it is not the general answer to this question.

E3A11.hint: Tropospheric ducting commonly stretches microwave contacts well beyond the normal horizon, but not to fantasy distances. The usual exam range is 100 to 300 miles.
E3A11.explanation: The correct answer is "100 miles to 300 miles" because tropospheric ducting can produce moderate but impressive microwave extensions beyond line of sight. Ten to fifty miles is too ordinary, while 1,200 or 2,500 miles are far beyond the typical value asked here.
E3A11.mnemonic: Typical tropo duct range: about 100 to 300 miles.
why_wrong: 10 to 50 miles is too short and close to ordinary line-of-sight expectations.
1,200 miles is much longer than the usual textbook duct range in this question.
2,500 miles is also far beyond the typical value being tested.

E3A12.hint: Auroral propagation shows up when geomagnetic conditions are strongly disturbed. If you see aurora in a propagation question, think severe geomagnetic storms.
E3A12.explanation: The correct answer is "Severe geomagnetic storms" because auroral propagation is tied to strong geomagnetic disturbance and energetic particles entering the auroral regions. Quiet conditions do the opposite and usually do not produce auroral propagation.
E3A12.mnemonic: Aurora on the air follows severe geomagnetic storms.
why_wrong: Meteor showers support meteor scatter, not auroral propagation.
Quiet geomagnetic conditions are the opposite of what typically creates auroral propagation.
Polar weather systems are not the direct cause of auroral radio propagation.

E3A13.hint: Auroral signals are rough, raspy, and distorted, which makes simple narrow modes work best. CW is the classic answer for auroral propagation.
E3A13.explanation: The correct answer is "CW" because auroral propagation often severely distorts voice and more complex modulations. CW remains readable under those harsh, buzzy conditions better than SSB, FM, or RTTY.
E3A13.mnemonic: For aurora, simple wins: use CW.
why_wrong: SSB usually sounds very distorted on auroral paths and is less effective than CW.
FM is not the preferred mode for typical auroral work.
RTTY is also more vulnerable to auroral distortion than CW.

E3A14.hint: Circular polarization means the field orientation rotates as the wave travels. So the best description is rotating electric and magnetic fields.
E3A14.explanation: The correct answer is "Waves with rotating electric and magnetic fields" because circular polarization describes a wave whose field vector rotates through time, tracing a circle. It does not mean the wave physically circles Earth or that it must come from a loop antenna.
why_wrong: The field is not literally bent into a circular shape.
Circular polarization is not about orbiting around the Earth.
Loop antennas can produce certain polarizations, but that is not the definition of circular polarization.

E3B01.hint: Transequatorial propagation works best on paths that cross the geomagnetic equator at right angles, with the endpoints a few thousand miles apart. Think 2,000 to 3,000 miles and perpendicular to the geomagnetic equator.
E3B01.explanation: The correct answer is "Between points separated by 2,000 miles to 3,000 miles over a path perpendicular to the geomagnetic equator" because TEP is a specific ionospheric mode tied to equatorial ionization regions. It is not simply any equatorial path or a terminator effect.
E3B01.mnemonic: TEP likes a few-thousand-mile path crossing the geomagnetic equator sideways.
why_wrong: A path lying on the geomagnetic equator is not the classic TEP geometry described here.
Antipodes are much farther apart and do not describe normal TEP geometry.
The terminator idea is a different propagation clue, not the definition of TEP.

E3B02.hint: TEP can reach farther than ordinary regional skip, but not halfway around the globe. The exam maximum to remember is about 5,000 miles.
E3B02.explanation: The correct answer is "5,000 miles" because transequatorial propagation can support very long VHF paths, but the usual maximum quoted in the question pool is around 5,000 miles.
E3B02.mnemonic: TEP maximum to memorize: about 5,000 miles.
why_wrong: 1,000 miles is too short for the approximate TEP maximum.
2,500 miles is within possible TEP distances but is not the maximum asked here.
7,500 miles is beyond the standard maximum value tested in the pool.

E3B03.hint: TEP most often peaks later in the day, especially afternoon into early evening. That is the common timing to remember.
E3B03.explanation: The correct answer is "Afternoon or early evening" because transequatorial propagation is most likely during those hours, when the equatorial ionosphere is favorable.
E3B03.mnemonic: TEP tends to show in the afternoon and early evening.
why_wrong: Morning is too early for the most likely TEP period.
Noon is closer, but the classic answer peaks later in the day.
Late at night is usually too late for the strongest typical TEP conditions.

E3B04.hint: Ordinary and extraordinary waves are the two separate ionospheric propagation components created by the Earth's magnetic field. They travel independently and are elliptically polarized.
E3B04.explanation: The correct answer is "Independently propagating, elliptically polarized waves created in the ionosphere" because ionized media in a magnetic field split radio waves into ordinary and extraordinary components.
E3B04.mnemonic: O and X waves are ionospheric split waves with their own polarization.
why_wrong: This invents a skip-distance distinction that is not what ordinary and extraordinary waves mean.
Long-path and short-path are different routing concepts, not ordinary vs extraordinary waves.
Refracted rays vs reflected waves is too general and misses the ionospheric magneto-ionic meaning.

E3B05.hint: For 160 meters, darkness is your friend because daytime D-layer absorption is a killer on top band. The best long-distance path is one entirely in darkness.
E3B05.explanation: The correct answer is "A path entirely in darkness" because low-band HF, especially 160 meters, suffers heavily from daytime D-layer absorption. A fully dark path minimizes that absorption and gives the best chance for long-distance propagation.
E3B05.mnemonic: Top band DX likes darkness end to end.
why_wrong: A sunlight path increases daytime absorption and hurts 160-meter distance work.
High latitudes often bring more absorption and auroral problems, not the best 160-meter DX path.
North-south orientation by itself is not the main deciding factor; full darkness is.

E3B06.hint: Long-path propagation is most often noticed on the middle HF bands where global ionospheric paths are common and practical. The exam pair to remember is 40 and 20 meters.
E3B06.explanation: The correct answer is "40 meters and 20 meters" because those bands most often support reliable long-path contacts. The lower bands are usually too absorption-prone or noisy, while 10 and 6 meters are more tied to changing high-frequency openings than the classic long-path answer.
E3B06.mnemonic: Long path shows up most often on 40 and 20 meters.
why_wrong: 160 and 80 meters are not the most frequent long-path pair in the exam answer set.
10 and 6 meters can open dramatically, but they are not the most frequent long-path pair here.
6 and 2 meters are too high in frequency for the standard long-path answer.

E3B07.hint: A lower elevation angle sends the wave farther out before it comes back down, so each ionospheric hop gets longer. Lower angle means longer skip distance.
E3B07.explanation: The correct answer is "The distance covered by each hop increases" because shallower launch angles intersect the ionosphere farther away, stretching the skip distance. Lowering the angle does not directly change MUF or critical frequency in the way the distractors suggest.
E3B07.mnemonic: Lower takeoff angle means longer skip hops.
why_wrong: Faraday rotation is not the main effect of changing launch angle in this question.
The MUF does not decrease just because you lowered the transmitted elevation angle.
Critical frequency is an ionospheric property and is not increased by lowering your takeoff angle.

E3B08.hint: Ground-wave range falls as frequency goes up. Lower frequencies hug the Earth better, so increasing frequency shortens ground-wave reach.
E3B08.explanation: The correct answer is "It decreases" because ground-wave propagation is stronger and travels farther at lower frequencies. As frequency rises, the ground-wave component attenuates more quickly and the useful range drops.
E3B08.mnemonic: Higher frequency means shorter ground-wave range.
why_wrong: Ground-wave range does not stay constant as frequency changes.
It does not increase; higher frequencies generally reduce ground-wave distance.
There is no special 8 MHz peak rule being tested here.

E3B09.hint: Sporadic-E is strongest seasonally around the solstices, especially in late spring and summer. So the exam answer centers on the solstices, with emphasis on the summer solstice.
E3B09.explanation: The correct answer is "Around the solstices, especially the summer solstice" because sporadic-E has a strong seasonal peak around that period. The equinoxes are not the classic strongest-season answer.
E3B09.mnemonic: Sporadic-E peaks near the solstices, especially summer.
why_wrong: Winter can have some Es, but the classic strongest answer is summer-season around the solstice.
Spring equinox is not the standard seasonal peak tested here.
Fall equinox is also not the classic peak season for sporadic-E.

E3B10.hint: Chordal-hop skips the lossy ground reflections between ionospheric hops, so the signal can travel with less loss than ordinary multi-hop paths. That reduction in loss is the key effect.
E3B10.explanation: The correct answer is "The signal experiences less loss compared to multi-hop propagation, which uses Earth as a reflector" because chordal-hop propagation bounces within the ionosphere without each hop returning to the Earth between refractions. Avoiding repeated ground reflections reduces loss.
E3B10.mnemonic: Chordal hop cuts out the lossy ground bounces.
why_wrong: The main effect is not a drastically lower MUF.
Noise reduction is not the defining effect being tested here.
Signals do not travel faster just because the path is chordal-hop.

E3B11.hint: Daytime is the classic daily window for sporadic-E. The best exam answer is between sunrise and sunset.
E3B11.explanation: The correct answer is "Between sunrise and sunset" because sporadic-E is most likely during daylight hours. Nighttime can have openings, but the typical daily period in the pool answer is daytime.
E3B11.mnemonic: Sporadic-E usually shows up in daylight.
why_wrong: Midnight to sunrise is too late for the most likely daily Es period.
Sunset to midnight is also outside the best general daytime answer.
All-night wording is too broad and misses the usual daylight peak.

E3B12.hint: Chordal-hop means the signal is refracted by the ionosphere multiple times without coming back to Earth between hops. That is the defining geometry.
E3B12.explanation: The correct answer is "Successive ionospheric refractions without an intermediate reflection from the ground" because chordal-hop stays aloft between ionospheric interactions instead of bouncing off the Earth each time.
E3B12.mnemonic: Chordal hop stays up between ionospheric bends.
why_wrong: This describes off-great-circle behavior, not chordal-hop.
Crossing the geomagnetic equator is transequatorial propagation, not chordal-hop.
Returning toward the transmitter is not the chordal-hop definition.

E3B13.hint: Ground-wave propagation favors vertical polarization because horizontal polarization is strongly canceled by ground interaction near the surface.
E3B13.explanation: The correct answer is "Vertical" because vertically polarized waves support ground-wave propagation much better. Horizontal components are attenuated more heavily by the Earth's surface.
why_wrong: Horizontal polarization is not the preferred ground-wave polarization.
Circular polarization is not the standard answer for ground-wave support.
Elliptical is also not the classic ground-wave polarization answer.

E3C01.hint: Short-term HF blackouts are flare events. A flare blasts out X-rays and UV that suddenly over-ionize the D layer, and the D layer absorbs signals instead of helping them.
E3C01.explanation: The correct answer is "Solar flares" because flares can trigger sudden ionospheric disturbances within minutes. That produces short-term radio blackouts, especially on HF daylight paths.
E3C01.mnemonic: Flare = fast blackout. CMEs are the slower storm makers.
why_wrong: Coronal mass ejections are more associated with geomagnetic storms that arrive later, not the immediate blackout effect.
Sunspots are activity markers, but a sunspot itself is not the direct cause of a short-term radio blackout.
A north-oriented interplanetary magnetic field is not the classic blackout trigger being tested here.

E3C02.hint: A-index and K-index are geomagnetic-disturbance numbers. If they are rising, the geomagnetic field is getting more disturbed, not quieter.
E3C02.explanation: The correct answer is "Increasing disturbance of the geomagnetic field" because both the A-index and K-index are measures of geomagnetic upset. Higher values mean worse disturbed-field conditions.
E3C02.mnemonic: A and K are agitation numbers.
why_wrong: That says the field is getting calmer, which is the opposite of what rising A or K means.
UV radiation is a solar metric, but A and K are specifically geomagnetic-disturbance indexes.
Critical frequency is not what A-index or K-index directly reports.

E3C03.hint: High A or K means auroral-region absorption gets ugly. Paths through the auroral oval are the ones most likely to be heavily absorbed.
E3C03.explanation: The correct answer is "Through the auroral oval" because geomagnetic disturbance strongly affects polar and auroral paths. Those paths often suffer severe absorption and degraded HF performance during storms.
why_wrong: Transequatorial propagation is a different path type and is not the classic high-absorption answer for elevated A or K.
Sporadic-E is not defined by auroral absorption the way polar and auroral paths are.
NVIS can be affected by conditions, but the auroral oval is the best match for unusually high absorption.

E3C04.hint: Bz is the north-south component of the interplanetary magnetic field. When people discuss Bz turning southward, they mean that north-south piece.
E3C04.explanation: The correct answer is "North-south strength of the interplanetary magnetic field" because Bz names the z-axis component of the IMF. In space-weather discussions, that north-south orientation matters a lot for geomagnetic coupling.
E3C04.mnemonic: Bz = Z-axis, the north-south part that matters.
why_wrong: Bz is not a direct measurement of geomagnetic-field stability.
Critical frequency is an ionospheric concept, not what Bz represents.
Long-delayed echoes are unrelated to the meaning of Bz.

E3C05.hint: Southward Bz is the trouble orientation. That alignment couples energy into Earth's field more effectively and raises the chance of disturbed conditions.
E3C05.explanation: The correct answer is "Southward" because a southward interplanetary magnetic field more easily reconnects with Earth's field. That increases the likelihood of geomagnetic disturbance.
why_wrong: Northward Bz is generally less favorable for strong geomagnetic coupling.
East-west wording is not the key orientation for this question.
Westward wording is also not the key orientation being tested.

E3C06.hint: Radio horizon is a little farther than visual horizon because atmospheric refraction bends VHF and UHF waves slightly. The exam number to remember is about 15 percent farther.
E3C06.explanation: The correct answer is "It is approximately 15 percent farther" because normal atmospheric refraction extends the effective VHF/UHF horizon beyond the purely geometric line-of-sight horizon.
why_wrong: The radio horizon is not nearer; refraction makes it a bit farther.
Fifty percent is much too large for the standard exam approximation.
They are close, but not the same; radio horizon is slightly farther.

E3C07.hint: Solar flare classes grow upward as A, B, C, M, X. `X` is the strongest standard class in that sequence.
E3C07.explanation: The correct answer is "Class X" because X-class flares are more intense than M, C, B, or A classes. If you just need the biggest flare label, choose X.
E3C07.mnemonic: X marks the extreme flare class.
why_wrong: Class A is one of the weakest flare categories, not the strongest.
Class Z is not the standard flare class used in this system.
Class M is strong, but X is stronger.

E3C08.hint: Geomagnetic storms use the `G` scale, and the most extreme category is `G5`.
E3C08.explanation: The correct answer is "G5" because NOAA geomagnetic storms are labeled G1 through G5. G5 is the extreme end of that scale.
why_wrong: B9 is not the geomagnetic-storm severity label used here.
X5 is a flare label, not the geomagnetic-storm category name.
M9 is also flare-style wording, not the geomagnetic storm term.

E3C09.hint: Propagation reporting networks like PSK Reporter watch actual received amateur signals. They are about who heard which digital or CW signal, not direct solar or magnetic measurements.
E3C09.explanation: The correct answer is "Digital-mode and CW signals" because amateur propagation reporting systems collect reception reports from stations using weak-signal digital modes and CW. They infer band conditions from those signal reports.
E3C09.mnemonic: Reporter networks report radio signals, not solar instruments.
why_wrong: Solar flux comes from space-weather data sources, not amateur reporting networks.
Electric field intensity is not the normal crowd-sourced data these networks publish.
Magnetic declination is unrelated to what propagation reporting networks track.

E3C10.hint: The `304A` parameter literally refers to ultraviolet emission around 304 angstroms. If one answer mentions UV at 304 angstroms, that is the direct match.
E3C10.explanation: The correct answer is "UV emissions at 304 angstroms, correlated to the solar flux index" because that is exactly what the 304A space-weather parameter describes.
why_wrong: That invents a ratio measurement that is not what the 304A parameter means.
This turns 304 into an angle and wind metric, which is not the meaning of the parameter.
304A is not a 304 GHz radio-frequency measurement.

E3C11.hint: VOACAP is an HF prediction tool. Think ionospheric path forecasts, MUF-style planning, and band-opening estimates.
E3C11.explanation: The correct answer is "HF propagation" because VOACAP models ionospheric propagation performance on HF paths. It is used to predict likely communication reliability by time, band, and path.
why_wrong: AC voltage and impedance are circuit topics, not what VOACAP models.
VOACAP is not mainly a VHF line-of-sight propagation model.
AC current and impedance are also unrelated to the software's purpose.

E3C12.hint: A sudden wideband jump in HF background noise points to a solar-radiation event or CME impact, not a nice propagation opening. Big solar disturbances often sound like the band noise floor rose everywhere.
E3C12.explanation: The correct answer is "A coronal mass ejection impact or a solar flare has occurred" because major solar events can raise HF noise and absorption across broad portions of the band at once.
E3C12.mnemonic: Sudden band-wide HF noise often means solar trouble, not DX magic.
why_wrong: A temperature inversion is more associated with VHF/UHF tropospheric effects, not a broad HF noise rise.
Transequatorial propagation is a specific opening type, not the normal cause of sudden HF-wide noise increase.
Long-path propagation may change signal routes, but it does not usually cause this broad sudden noise jump.

E4A01.hint: A digital scope cannot faithfully show frequencies above what its ADC can sample well. If the samples come too slowly, the display lies to you.
E4A01.explanation: The correct answer is "Sampling rate of the analog-to-digital converter" because a digital oscilloscope depends on its ADC taking enough samples per second to reconstruct the waveform accurately. Too low a sample rate causes aliasing and loss of detail.
E4A01.mnemonic: Digital scope truth starts with sample rate.
why_wrong: The ADC reference frequency is not the main limit on the highest waveform frequency it can display accurately.
Circuit Q may matter in a tuned circuit, but it does not set the scope's display bandwidth limit.
Since the sample rate is the key limit here, `all` is too broad.

E4A02.hint: A spectrum analyzer is amplitude versus frequency. A scope is amplitude versus time. That one comparison answers a lot of test questions.
E4A02.explanation: The correct answer is "Signal amplitude and frequency" because the vertical axis of a spectrum analyzer shows level, while the horizontal axis shows frequency position.
E4A02.mnemonic: Spectrum = how much signal at which frequency.
why_wrong: Amplitude versus time describes an oscilloscope display, not a spectrum analyzer.
SWR is not what a standard spectrum analyzer uses for its main axes.
This combines two wrong axes for a spectrum analyzer.

E4A03.hint: Spurious outputs and IMD products are extra signals at extra frequencies. The right tool is the one that shows signal level across frequency.
E4A03.explanation: The correct answer is "Spectrum analyzer" because it lets you see unwanted outputs, harmonics, and intermodulation products as separate peaks in the frequency domain.
E4A03.mnemonic: Spurs live in the spectrum, so use a spectrum analyzer.
why_wrong: A differential resolver is not the instrument used to inspect RF spurious outputs.
A logic analyzer is for digital timing states, not RF spectral purity.
A network analyzer measures device behavior like impedance and S-parameters, not transmitter spur displays as directly as a spectrum analyzer.

E4A04.hint: Probe compensation uses a square-wave calibrator. You tweak the probe until the square wave top is flat, not rounded and not peaky.
E4A04.explanation: The correct answer is "A square wave is displayed, and the probe is adjusted until the horizontal portions of the displayed wave are as nearly flat as possible" because proper compensation removes under- and over-compensation errors in the displayed square wave.
E4A04.mnemonic: Compensate with a square wave until the top is square.
why_wrong: Maximum sine-wave amplitude is not the probe-compensation method.
Probe compensation is not done by timing a frequency standard.
It is also not done by adjusting for a DC voltage reading.

E4A05.hint: A prescaler is a divider. It knocks a very high frequency down so the counter can handle it.
E4A05.explanation: The correct answer is "Reduce the signal frequency to within the counter's operating range" because a prescaler divides the input frequency before it reaches the counter's counting circuits.
why_wrong: A prescaler does not exist mainly to amplify weak signals.
It divides down; it does not multiply the signal upward.
Preventing oscillation is not the prescaler's main purpose.

E4A06.hint: Aliasing means the scope invents a fake lower-frequency pattern because it did not sample fast enough. The waveform you see may look stable-ish but be completely wrong.
E4A06.explanation: The correct answer is "A false, jittery low-frequency version of the waveform is displayed" because undersampling creates an alias, which is an incorrect lower-frequency representation of the real signal.
E4A06.mnemonic: Aliasing is a fake low-frequency ghost.
why_wrong: DC offset error is not the main signature of aliasing.
Aliasing does not mean the vertical calibration itself has failed.
Blanking is not the usual issue; the issue is a misleading displayed waveform.

E4A07.hint: An SWR bridge mainly tells you mismatch. An antenna analyzer usually gives you more, including impedance details and computed SWR without needing the transmitter on the air.
E4A07.explanation: The correct answer is "Antenna analyzers compute SWR and impedance automatically" because that is a practical advantage over a simple SWR bridge. It gives a fuller picture of the load.
E4A07.mnemonic: Analyzer adds impedance information, not just SWR.
why_wrong: Most antenna analyzers measure and report; they do not automatically retune the antenna for you.
Displaying a time-varying modulation envelope is scope behavior, not the antenna analyzer's purpose.
Since the first and third options are wrong, `all` cannot be correct.

E4A08.hint: Several different instruments can measure SWR. A directional wattmeter can infer it from forward/reflected power, and analyzers or VNAs can compute it directly.
E4A08.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because a directional wattmeter, vector network analyzer, and antenna analyzer can all be used to measure or determine SWR.
E4A08.mnemonic: If it can compare mismatch behavior, it can get you SWR.
why_wrong: A directional wattmeter can measure forward and reflected power, so by itself it is a valid SWR tool.
A vector network analyzer can absolutely determine SWR from reflection data.
An antenna analyzer also measures SWR, so this single option is incomplete rather than wrong in principle.

E4A09.hint: Keep the probe ground lead short. A long ground lead adds inductance and can distort fast or RF-related measurements badly.
E4A09.explanation: The correct answer is "Minimize the length of the probe's ground connection" because shorter ground connections reduce stray inductance and pickup, improving measurement accuracy.
why_wrong: High-impedance probes are commonly used precisely to avoid loading many circuits, including low-impedance points in many cases.
A DC-coupled probe can still measure AC signals; coupling choice depends on what part of the waveform you want to see.
Since the middle two statements are not good blanket rules, `all` is wrong.

E4A10.hint: Power-supply ripple rides on the AC mains frequency, so line triggering is the natural choice when you want a stable display of that ripple.
E4A10.explanation: The correct answer is "Line" because line trigger synchronizes the oscilloscope to the power-line cycle that produces the ripple in a linear power supply.
why_wrong: Single-shot is for one-time events, not repetitive line-frequency ripple.
Edge triggering can work in many cases, but line trigger is the most direct and effective choice for this specific task.
Level trigger is a broad trigger setting, but line trigger is the purpose-built answer here.

E4A11.hint: Modern antenna analyzers can do more than just SWR. Depending on the model, they can estimate cable length, velocity factor, and resonant points too.
E4A11.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because antenna analyzers can be used for velocity-factor work, cable-length estimation, and finding the resonant frequency of tuned circuits or antennas.
E4A11.mnemonic: Analyzer tools often do more than antennas alone.
why_wrong: Velocity factor can be measured or estimated, so this single answer is incomplete.
Cable length can also be determined on many analyzers, so this alone is not the whole answer.
Resonant frequency is measurable too, making this single option incomplete.

E4B01.hint: A frequency counter is only as good as its clock reference. If the time base is off, every frequency reading is off with it.
E4B01.explanation: The correct answer is "Time base accuracy" because the counter measures frequency by counting cycles against an internal time reference. A better time base means a more accurate reading.
why_wrong: Input attenuator accuracy matters much less than the stability and accuracy of the reference clock.
Divider stages matter, but the dominant accuracy limit is still the time base.
Logic temperature effects exist, but they are not the main factor compared with the time reference.

E4B02.hint: Ohms-per-volt is an old analog-meter loading spec. Multiply the full-scale voltage range by the ohms-per-volt rating to get the meter's input resistance on that range.
E4B02.explanation: The correct answer is "The full scale reading of the voltmeter multiplied by its ohms per volt rating is the input impedance of the voltmeter" because that is how analog voltmeter sensitivity translates into meter loading.
E4B02.mnemonic: Ohms per volt times full-scale volts gives input ohms.
why_wrong: That is not how meter sensitivity is used, and it does not directly tell you DUT power draw.
Dividing an ohmic reading by ohms-per-volt is not the meaning of the sensitivity rating.
This has nothing to do with shunt sizing in current measurement.

E4B03.hint: Think `output over input` for forward gain in a two-port device. Signal goes in port 1 and comes out port 2, so that is `S21`.
E4B03.explanation: The correct answer is "S21" because S-parameters use the first subscript for the output port and the second for the driven input port. Forward transmission from port 1 to port 2 is S21.
why_wrong: S11 is input reflection, not forward gain.
S12 is reverse transmission, not forward gain.
S22 is output-port reflection, not forward transmission.

E4B04.hint: If you are looking at how much of the signal reflects back at the input port, both subscripts stay `1`: that is `S11`.
E4B04.explanation: The correct answer is "S11" because input return loss and reflection coefficient are measured at port 1 with port 1 as the excited/reference port.
why_wrong: S12 is reverse transmission between ports, not input return loss.
S21 is forward gain or transmission, not reflection.
S22 is output-port reflection, not input-port reflection.

E4B05.hint: Classic VNA calibration is SOL: short, open, load. The load is the system impedance, here 50 ohms.
E4B05.explanation: The correct answer is "Short circuit, open circuit, and 50 ohms" because those three standards let the VNA calibrate reflection measurements at the test port.
why_wrong: These are just resistance values, not the standard calibration references.
A resonant circuit is not one of the standard basic calibration loads here.
Transmission-line lengths are not the basic three calibration standards being asked about.

E4B06.hint: Power absorbed by the load is forward minus reflected. Here that is `100 - 25 = 75 watts`.
E4B06.explanation: The correct answer is "75 watts" because reflected power is not absorbed by the load. The load absorbs the net forward power after subtracting the reflected portion.
why_wrong: That ignores the 25 watts reflected back from the load.
You do not add forward and reflected power to get absorbed power.
There is no averaging step here; just subtract reflected from forward.

E4B07.hint: In `S21`, the subscripts tell you which ports are involved. S-parameter numbers are about port relationships, not time or data quality.
E4B07.explanation: The correct answer is "The port or ports at which measurements are made" because the subscripts identify the output and input ports associated with the parameter.
why_wrong: The subscripts do not indicate timing.
They also do not rate the quality of the measurement.
They are not a frequency-order label either.

E4B08.hint: Q tells you how sharp the tuning is. Sharp tuning means narrow bandwidth, so Q is tied directly to bandwidth.
E4B08.explanation: The correct answer is "The bandwidth of the circuit's frequency response" because Q for a tuned circuit is commonly determined from resonant frequency divided by bandwidth.
why_wrong: The ratio of XL to XC is not the standard direct way to determine loaded Q here.
Frequency shift by itself is not enough; bandwidth is the key quantity.
Resonant frequency alone does not tell you how sharp the circuit is.

E4B09.hint: A two-port VNA is built to measure how a device passes or rejects signals across frequency. That makes it perfect for filter response curves.
E4B09.explanation: The correct answer is "Filter frequency response" because a two-port vector network analyzer measures transmission and reflection versus frequency, which is exactly what you need for filters.
why_wrong: Phase noise is usually characterized with different specialized measurements.
Pulse rise time is more of a time-domain measurement than a standard two-port VNA task.
Forward power is not the main two-port VNA measurement asked here.

E4B10.hint: The classic SSB IMD test is a two-tone audio test. Use two non-harmonically related AF tones and look at the RF output on a spectrum analyzer.
E4B10.explanation: The correct answer is "Modulate the transmitter using two AF signals having non-harmonically related frequencies and observe the RF output with a spectrum analyzer" because that method reveals third-order and higher intermodulation products around the main SSB tones.
E4B10.mnemonic: SSB IMD test = two AF tones plus spectrum analyzer.
why_wrong: The standard IMD test for an SSB transmitter uses AF tones, not two RF drive signals in that way.
Harmonically related tones and a peak wattmeter do not properly reveal SSB intermod products.
A logic analyzer is the wrong tool, and harmonically related RF tones are not the standard method.

E4B11.hint: A VNA can characterize impedance and reflection behavior at a port, so it can provide input impedance, output impedance, and reflection coefficient information.
E4B11.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because a vector network analyzer can measure reflection and transmission data from which input impedance, output impedance, and reflection coefficient are obtained.
E4B11.mnemonic: VNA sees impedance and reflection from every side.
why_wrong: A VNA can measure input impedance, but that is only part of what it can do.
Output impedance is also measurable, so this single choice is incomplete.
Reflection coefficient is measurable too, making this choice incomplete by itself.

E4C01.hint: Excessive phase noise smears the local oscillator energy around nearby frequencies. Strong nearby signals can mix with that noise skirt and spill interference into the wanted channel.
E4C01.explanation: The correct answer is "It can combine with strong signals on nearby frequencies to generate interference" because poor master-clock phase noise in an SDR can create reciprocal-mixing type problems that raise noise and interference near strong adjacent signals.
E4C01.mnemonic: Phase-noise skirts plus strong neighbors equal interference.
why_wrong: Strong-signal handling is more directly tied to dynamic range and overload limits than to this specific phase-noise effect.
Frequency calibration can drift for other reasons, but the main problem tested here is nearby-signal interference.
Third-order intercept point is not literally decreased by phase noise; this question is about interference from noisy LO skirts.

E4C02.hint: Strong out-of-band signals should be stopped before they hit the sensitive receiver stages. That makes the front-end filter or preselector the right answer.
E4C02.explanation: The correct answer is "A front-end filter or preselector" because it rejects strong signals outside the tuned range before they can overload mixers, amplifiers, or ADC stages.
E4C02.mnemonic: Kill out-of-band trouble at the front end.
why_wrong: A narrow IF filter comes later in the chain and may not stop front-end overload from out-of-band signals.
A notch filter removes specific interferers, but it is not the general best answer for strong out-of-band rejection.
A product detector is not the cure for strong out-of-band front-end overload.

E4C03.hint: FM has a capture effect: when two signals are on the same frequency, the stronger one tends to win and suppress the weaker one in the receiver.
E4C03.explanation: The correct answer is "Capture effect" because FM receivers commonly lock onto the stronger of two co-channel signals, reducing or eliminating the weaker one.
E4C03.mnemonic: In FM, the stronger signal captures the receiver.
why_wrong: Desensitization is a broader overload problem, not the specific same-frequency FM effect.
Cross modulation is a different interference mechanism.
Frequency discrimination is part of FM detection, not the name of this stronger-signal-wins effect.

E4C04.hint: Noise figure tells you how much noisier the real receiver is than a perfect one. It is a penalty above the theoretical minimum noise.
E4C04.explanation: The correct answer is "The ratio in dB of the noise generated by the receiver to the theoretical minimum noise" because noise figure compares actual receiver-added noise to the ideal minimum thermal-noise baseline.
E4C04.mnemonic: Noise figure = how far above perfect the receiver is.
why_wrong: Atmospheric noise versus phase noise is not the definition of receiver noise figure.
Bandwidth ratio wording is not the standard NF definition.
Noise figure is not referenced to atmospheric noise in this way.

E4C05.hint: `-174 dBm/Hz` is the famous room-temperature thermal-noise floor number. Think ideal receiver input, 1 Hz bandwidth, room temperature.
E4C05.explanation: The correct answer is "The theoretical noise in a 1 Hz bandwidth at the input of a perfect receiver at room temperature" because `-174 dBm` is the standard thermal-noise reference per hertz at about 290 K.
E4C05.mnemonic: -174 dBm/Hz = thermal noise at room temperature.
why_wrong: This is not 6 dB above the theoretical minimum; it is the theoretical minimum reference itself.
It is not the noise figure of a receiver; it is the thermal-noise floor reference.
It is not 3 dB above minimum either.

E4C06.hint: Noise power rises with bandwidth. Going from `50 Hz` to `1000 Hz` is a `20x` increase, and `10 log10(20)` is about `13 dB`.
E4C06.explanation: The correct answer is "13 dB" because increasing bandwidth from 50 Hz to 1000 Hz multiplies noise bandwidth by 20, which raises the noise floor by about 13 dB.
E4C06.mnemonic: 20 times bandwidth adds about 13 dB noise.
why_wrong: 3 dB would only be a 2x power increase, far too small.
5 dB is still too small for a 20x bandwidth increase.
10 dB would match a 10x change, not 20x.

E4C07.hint: MDS literally stands for minimum discernible signal. It is the weakest signal the receiver can still detect meaningfully.
E4C07.explanation: The correct answer is "The minimum discernible signal" because MDS is the standard receiver-sensitivity term for the smallest usable input signal.
why_wrong: This invents a meter-display term that MDS does not mean.
MDS is not a distortion specification.
The phrase is minimum discernible signal, not maximum detectable spectrum.

E4C08.hint: An SDR ADC overloads when the input drives it beyond the voltage range it can convert. That range is set by the ADC reference voltage.
E4C08.explanation: The correct answer is "The reference voltage of the analog-to-digital converter" because once the signal exceeds the ADC input range tied to its reference voltage, clipping and overload occur.
E4C08.mnemonic: ADC overload happens when input outruns its voltage range.
why_wrong: Sample rate has nothing to do with the amplitude level that causes overload.
