E6E09.explanation: The correct answer is "Surface mount" because surface-mount packaging minimizes parasitic effects that become troublesome at higher frequencies.
E6E09.mnemonic: Shorter leads mean better high-frequency behavior.
why_wrong: A `TO-220` package has relatively long leads and more parasitics.
Axial-lead parts are worse at UHF because of lead inductance.
Radial-lead parts also have more unwanted parasitic reactance than surface mount.

E6E10.hint: All three listed RF advantages of surface mount are true: smaller area, shorter traces, and lower parasitic inductance/capacitance. When every one helps at RF, pick `all these choices`.
E6E10.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because surface-mount technology improves RF layout in all of the listed ways.
E6E10.mnemonic: Surface mount helps everywhere at RF, so 'all' is the clue.
why_wrong: Smaller circuit area is true, but it is only one part of the full answer.
Shorter traces are also true, but not the whole story.
Lower parasitics are true too, but the question includes multiple correct benefits.

E6E11.hint: `DIP` literally stands for dual in-line package, so look for the choice describing two parallel rows of pins on opposite sides.
E6E11.explanation: The correct answer is "Two rows of connecting pins on opposite sides of package (dual in-line package)" because that is exactly what the term dual in-line package means.
why_wrong: That expands `DIP` incorrectly and describes a property, not the package shape.
This also invents a false expansion of `DIP`.
Dual in-line does not mean two chips in one package.

E6E12.hint: Through-hole `DIP` ICs are poor at UHF mainly because their leads are too long. Those leads act like little inductors and add stray effects that ruin high-frequency performance.
E6E12.explanation: The correct answer is "Excessive lead length" because long DIP leads create parasitic inductance and capacitance that become significant at UHF and higher frequencies.
why_wrong: Dielectric loss is not the main reason DIP packages are avoided here.
The epoxy coating is not the issue.
The problem is RF parasitics from the package, not mixing analog and digital functions.

E6F01.hint: Light arrives as photons, but the energy is absorbed by electrons in the photovoltaic material. Those energized electrons are what make current possible.
E6F01.explanation: The correct answer is "Electrons" because in a photovoltaic cell, electrons absorb the incoming light energy and are promoted to higher energy states, allowing charge flow.
why_wrong: Protons are not the mobile charge carriers involved in the photovoltaic effect.
Photons deliver the light energy, but they are not the charges that absorb it within the cell material in the sense tested here.
Holes matter in semiconductor behavior, but the pool answer for absorbed light energy is electrons.

E6F02.hint: Photoconductive material becomes more conductive in light because more charge carriers are available. More conductivity means less resistance.
E6F02.explanation: The correct answer is "Resistance decreases" because illumination increases the number of carriers in a photoconductive material.
why_wrong: The opposite happens: light generally lowers the resistance of photoconductive material.
The question is about electrical conductivity, not reflectivity.
Reflectivity is not the effect being tested here.

E6F03.hint: An optoisolator usually has a light source on one side and a light-sensitive device on the other. The standard pair is an `LED` driving a phototransistor.
E6F03.explanation: The correct answer is "An LED and a phototransistor" because that is the most common optocoupler arrangement.
why_wrong: That is not the normal compact solid-state optocoupler construction.
Helium-neon laser systems are unrelated to the usual optoisolator package.
This is also not how ordinary optocouplers are built.

E6F04.hint: Photovoltaic means light creates voltage and current. In plain language, it is the conversion of light into electrical energy.
E6F04.explanation: The correct answer is "The conversion of light to electrical energy" because that is the definition of the photovoltaic effect.
why_wrong: That reverses the idea. The photovoltaic effect converts light into electricity, not voltage into current.
A photodiode normally detects light; this is not the definition of the photovoltaic effect.
Phototransistor beta change is not the concept being asked.

E6F05.hint: An optical shaft encoder uses a slotted or patterned wheel to break a light beam as something rotates. The pulses tell you position or speed.
E6F05.explanation: The correct answer is "A device that detects rotation by interrupting a light source with a patterned wheel" because that is how optical shaft encoders sense motion.
E6F05.mnemonic: Spinning wheel chops light into pulses.
why_wrong: This describes a light meter, not a shaft encoder.
Fiber-optic computing is unrelated to the rotating-wheel sensor idea.
RTTY generation is not what an optical shaft encoder does.

E6F06.hint: Photoconductive devices are usually made from semiconductor materials whose conductivity changes with light. That makes `crystalline semiconductor` the right category answer.
E6F06.explanation: The correct answer is "Crystalline semiconductor" because photoconductive devices depend on semiconductor carrier generation by light.
why_wrong: This is not a standard photoconductive material class.
Argon is a gas, not the typical solid photoconductive device material.
Not all of the choices are appropriate materials for photoconductive devices.

E6F07.hint: A solid-state relay does the switching job of a relay without moving contacts. It uses semiconductor devices to imitate relay behavior electronically.
E6F07.explanation: The correct answer is "A device that uses semiconductors to implement the functions of an electromechanical relay" because that is exactly what a solid-state relay is.
E6F07.mnemonic: Solid-state relay = relay job, semiconductor guts.
why_wrong: Using transistors to drive a coil still leaves you with a mechanical relay, not a solid-state relay.
A latching relay is still mechanical.
This is too narrow and describes a specific circuit idea, not the general definition.

E6F08.hint: The whole point of an optoisolator is isolation: light crosses the gap, but direct electrical connection does not. That protects low-voltage control circuitry from `120 VAC` switching circuits.
E6F08.explanation: The correct answer is "Optoisolators provide an electrical isolation between a control circuit and the circuit being switched" because optical coupling allows signal transfer without direct conductive connection.
why_wrong: Low impedance is not the goal of an optoisolator.
Impedance matching is not the purpose either.
Reflected light is not the issue being addressed in AC control isolation.

E6F09.hint: PV efficiency asks how much of the incoming light energy turns into useful electrical output. In this pool wording, look for the fraction of light converted to current.
E6F09.explanation: The correct answer is "The relative fraction of light that is converted to current" because photovoltaic efficiency is about converting incident light into electrical output.
E6F09.mnemonic: Efficiency = useful electricity out from light in.
why_wrong: RF power versus DC power is an amplifier efficiency idea, not a solar-cell one.
Lumens are a lighting-output measure, not the photovoltaic efficiency definition used here.
Open-circuit voltage over short-circuit current is not an efficiency formula.

E6F10.hint: Most power-producing solar cells are made from `silicon`. It is the standard mainstream photovoltaic material.
E6F10.explanation: The correct answer is "Silicon" because silicon is the most common material used in practical power-generating photovoltaic cells.
why_wrong: Selenium was used in older photo devices, but it is not the common modern power-cell material.
Cadmium sulfide is used in some light-sensitive devices, not the usual power-generating PV cells here.
Indium arsenide is not the standard answer for common photovoltaic power generation.

E6F11.hint: A single fully illuminated silicon solar cell produces about half a volt open-circuit. Remember: one cell is roughly `0.5 V`, so panels stack many cells in series.
E6F11.explanation: The correct answer is "0.5 volts" because a silicon photovoltaic cell typically produces around half a volt open-circuit under illumination.
E6F11.mnemonic: One silicon cell, about half a volt.
why_wrong: `0.7 V` is more like a silicon diode turn-on memory number, not the usual solar-cell open-circuit value here.
`1.1 V` is too high for one ordinary silicon PV cell.
`1.5 V` is more like a dry cell battery, not a single silicon photovoltaic cell.

E7A01.hint: A bistable circuit has two stable states and can store one bit. That is exactly what a flip-flop does.
E7A01.explanation: The correct answer is "A flip-flop" because a flip-flop is the classic bistable circuit, with two stable output states.
E7A01.mnemonic: Bi-stable means two stable states, like a flip-flop's 0 and 1.
why_wrong: An AND gate is combinational logic, not a two-state memory element.
An OR gate is also combinational logic, not bistable storage.
A bipolar amplifier can amplify, but it is not inherently a bistable circuit.

E7A02.hint: A decade counter counts ten states, so it divides the input pulse stream by 10. After ten input pulses, the cycle repeats.
E7A02.explanation: The correct answer is "It produces one output pulse for every 10 input pulses" because a decade counter is a divide-by-10 counter.
E7A02.mnemonic: Decade means ten, so think divide by 10.
why_wrong: Decoding for a display is a decoder function, not the main job of the counter itself.
A decade counter does not create ten outputs per input pulse.
Binary-to-display decoding is a different circuit function.

E7A03.hint: A flip-flop toggles state on clock pulses, so one full output cycle takes two input pulses. That makes it a divide-by-2 circuit.
E7A03.explanation: The correct answer is "A flip-flop" because a toggling flip-flop divides an input pulse train frequency by 2.
why_wrong: An XOR gate performs logic comparison, not simple divide-by-2 counting by itself.
An OR gate is not a frequency divider.
A multiplexer selects among inputs; it does not normally divide pulse frequency.

E7A04.hint: Each flip-flop divides by 2. To divide by 16, stack enough stages so `2 x 2 x 2 x 2 = 16`, which takes four flip-flops.
E7A04.explanation: The correct answer is "4" because four divide-by-2 stages in series produce an overall divide-by-16 result.
why_wrong: Six flip-flops would divide by `2^6 = 64`, not 16.
Eight flip-flops would divide by `256`.
Sixteen flip-flops would be far more than needed.

E7A05.hint: An astable multivibrator never settles in either state. It keeps switching back and forth on its own, creating a continuous waveform without an external clock.
E7A05.explanation: The correct answer is "Astable multivibrator" because astable circuits continuously alternate between states by themselves.
why_wrong: A monostable has one stable state and only makes a temporary transition.
A J-K flip-flop normally needs clocking and is not the free-running oscillator answer here.
A T flip-flop also depends on clock pulses rather than oscillating by itself.

E7A06.hint: A monostable multivibrator has one stable state and one temporary state. Trigger it, and it changes state briefly, then returns after a fixed time.
E7A06.explanation: The correct answer is "It switches temporarily to an alternate state for a set time" because that is the defining behavior of a monostable multivibrator, also called a one-shot.
E7A06.mnemonic: Mono-stable = one stable state, one timed pulse.
why_wrong: A continuous square wave is astable behavior, not monostable.
Storing one bit describes a flip-flop, not a one-shot.
Maintaining constant voltage describes a regulator, not a monostable circuit.

E7A07.hint: A `NAND` gate is `NOT-AND`: an AND gate would go high only when all inputs are high, then the NOT reverses that. So the only time output becomes `0` is when all inputs are `1`.
E7A07.explanation: The correct answer is "It produces a 0 at its output only if all inputs are 1" because NAND is the inverted form of the AND function.
why_wrong: If all inputs are 0, a NAND output is 1, not 0.
If all inputs are 1, a NAND output is 0, not 1.
That describes an exclusive-type condition, not NAND logic.

E7A08.hint: An OR gate is inclusive: if this input or that input or both are `1`, the output is `1`. It only stays `0` when every input is `0`.
E7A08.explanation: The correct answer is "It produces a 1 at its output if any input is 1" because that is the basic OR truth rule.
E7A08.mnemonic: OR means any 1 makes the output 1.
why_wrong: That is not OR behavior; all inputs being 1 does not force the output to 0.
This describes an exclusive condition, not ordinary OR.
If all inputs are 0, an OR output is 0, not 1.

E7A09.hint: An `XNOR` gate is the inverse of `XOR`. `XOR` goes high when inputs differ, so `XNOR` goes low when exactly one input is `1` and the other is `0`.
E7A09.explanation: The correct answer is "It produces a 0 at its output if one and only one of its inputs is 1" because exclusive NOR is true when the two inputs match and false when they differ.
why_wrong: If both inputs are 0, an XNOR output is 1, not 0.
If both inputs are 1, an XNOR output is also 1, not the special low condition here.
That is XOR behavior, not XNOR.

E7A10.hint: A truth table is just a chart showing every input combination and the output that results. It is the standard way to describe digital logic behavior.
E7A10.explanation: The correct answer is "A list of inputs and corresponding outputs for a digital device" because that is exactly what a truth table contains.
E7A10.mnemonic: Truth table tells the true output for each input pattern.
why_wrong: Truth tables describe digital logic, not op-amp transfer behavior.
A truth table is not limited to rows where the output is true.
It is about input-output relationships, not a table of graphic symbols.

E7A11.hint: Positive logic means the higher voltage level is assigned logical `1`, and the lower level is logical `0`. That is the common digital convention.
E7A11.explanation: The correct answer is "High voltage represents a 1, low voltage a 0" because that is the definition of positive logic.
E7A11.mnemonic: Positive logic puts 1 on the higher level.
why_wrong: Noise immunity is a separate performance trait, not the definition of positive logic.
Being in a 'true' condition is not the meaning of the term positive logic.
Positive logic does not mean both 1 and 0 are positive voltages; it means the higher level maps to 1.

E7B01.hint: Class `AB` sits between `A` and `B`: each device conducts for a little more than half the cycle, but not all of it. That avoids the severe crossover issue of pure Class B without burning as much power as Class A.
E7B01.explanation: The correct answer is "More than 180 degrees but less than 360 degrees" because each active device in a push-pull Class AB amplifier conducts for slightly more than half of the waveform.
E7B01.mnemonic: AB is above B's 180 degrees, below A's full 360.
why_wrong: Exactly 180 degrees is the ideal Class B case.
The entire cycle is Class A behavior.
Less than 180 degrees points toward Class C operation.

E7B02.hint: A Class `D` amplifier works by switching devices hard on and off instead of holding them in the linear region. That switching approach is why it can be very efficient.
E7B02.explanation: The correct answer is "An amplifier that uses switching technology to achieve high efficiency" because Class D amplifiers operate their active devices as switches.
why_wrong: Class D is not defined as a low-power differential linear amplifier.
Drift-mode FET wording is not the defining characteristic here.
Being biased for low distortion describes linear classes more than Class D switching.

E7B03.hint: RF switching amplifiers create square-like waveforms rich in harmonics, so the output needs filtering before the signal goes to the antenna.
E7B03.explanation: The correct answer is "A filter to remove harmonic content" because switching amplifiers generate substantial harmonics that must be suppressed.
E7B03.mnemonic: Switching makes harmonics, filters clean them up.
why_wrong: A high-pass filter is not used here to fix low-frequency gain.
A load resistor is not the main harmonic-cleanup requirement.
Temperature compensation is not the output-stage issue being tested.

E7B04.hint: For Class `A`, you bias the transistor in the middle of the linear region so the signal can swing both ways without clipping. That means near the midpoint between cutoff and saturation.
E7B04.explanation: The correct answer is "Approximately halfway between saturation and cutoff" because a Class A common-emitter amplifier is biased around the center of its load line for symmetrical signal swing.
E7B04.mnemonic: Class A aims for the middle of the active region.
why_wrong: Emitter and base voltage midpoint is not the standard way this operating point is defined.
Equal resistor values do not define the correct quiescent point by themselves.
The zero-bias current intercept is not the usual Class A operating point.

E7B05.hint: Unwanted oscillations in RF power amplifiers are usually tamed by suppressing parasitics and reducing stray feedback. Neutralization and parasitic suppressors are the classic fixes.
E7B05.explanation: The correct answer is "Install parasitic suppressors and/or neutralize the stage" because those measures reduce self-oscillation caused by stray reactance and feedback.
E7B05.mnemonic: Parasitics and feedback cause trouble; suppress or neutralize them.
why_wrong: Tuning for minimum loading is not the standard cure for parasitic oscillation.
Tuning for maximum power does not solve the stability problem.
A phase inverter is not the normal remedy for RF parasitics.

E7B06.hint: A grounded-grid amplifier is known for relatively low input impedance, which is one reason it can be driven effectively from a low-impedance source.
E7B06.explanation: The correct answer is "Low input impedance" because grounded-grid tube amplifiers typically present a low input impedance at the cathode drive point.
why_wrong: Grounded-grid amplifiers are not chosen because they have especially high power gain.
Electrostatic damage protection is not the defining characteristic here.
Low bandwidth is also not the main identifier of grounded-grid operation.

E7B07.hint: Class `C` is efficient for constant-envelope signals like CW or FM, not linear voice modes like `SSB`. Using it on SSB badly distorts the envelope and splatters signal energy wider than it should.
E7B07.explanation: The correct answer is "Signal distortion and excessive bandwidth" because a Class C amplifier is too nonlinear to preserve a single-sideband phone signal.
E7B07.mnemonic: Class C and SSB do not mix.
why_wrong: Class C does not reduce intermodulation for SSB; it makes linearity worse.
Severely distorted SSB does not improve intelligibility.
Third-order distortion is not reduced by using a nonlinear class for SSB.

E7B08.hint: Switching amplifiers are efficient because the device spends very little time half-on and dissipating power. It is mostly either fully on or fully off.
E7B08.explanation: The correct answer is "The switching device is at saturation or cutoff most of the time" because that minimizes power wasted in the active device.
E7B08.mnemonic: Fully on or fully off wastes less heat.
why_wrong: Higher voltage operation is not the key reason for the improved efficiency.
Linear amplifiers can have harmonic issues, but that is not why switching amplifiers are more efficient.
Push-pull layout alone does not explain the efficiency difference.

E7B09.hint: An emitter follower, also called a common-collector stage, follows the input voltage instead of inverting it. The voltage gain is near 1, but the phase stays the same.
E7B09.explanation: The correct answer is "Input and output signals in-phase" because a common-collector amplifier does not invert the signal.
E7B09.mnemonic: Emitter follower follows, so phase stays the same.
why_wrong: Emitter followers actually have high input impedance, not low.
Differential-input behavior describes a different amplifier type.
It is not a logic OR function.

E7B10.hint: In the figure, `R1` and `R2` form the classic base-bias divider that sets a stable DC operating point. Two resistors feeding the base usually mean voltage-divider bias.
E7B10.explanation: The correct answer is "Voltage divider bias" because `R1` and `R2` establish the transistor base bias with a resistor divider.
E7B10.mnemonic: Two base resistors usually mean divider bias.
why_wrong: They are not serving as the collector load.
Self bias in this circuit is associated with the emitter resistor, not the divider pair.
Although bias networks affect stability, the specific function here is divider bias.

E7B11.hint: An emitter resistor like `R3` develops voltage from the transistor's own current, which automatically helps set and stabilize bias. That is self-bias or emitter degeneration bias.
E7B11.explanation: The correct answer is "Self bias" because `R3` creates a bias voltage from the transistor's own emitter current.
E7B11.mnemonic: The transistor biases itself through the emitter resistor.
why_wrong: Fixed bias would come from a dedicated applied bias source rather than emitter feedback.
An emitter bypass is usually a capacitor, not the resistor itself.
The output load resistor is typically in the collector path, not this role.

E7B12.hint: With a collector load, emitter resistor, and signal taken from the collector, the figure is the standard common-emitter amplifier configuration.
E7B12.explanation: The correct answer is "Common emitter" because the emitter is the common reference point and the output is taken from the collector.
E7B12.mnemonic: Output at collector, emitter common: common-emitter.
why_wrong: In a common-base stage the base is the signal reference, which is not the case here.
A common-collector stage is an emitter follower, not this collector-output circuit.
Emitter follower is another name for common collector, not common emitter.

E7C01.hint: A low-pass `Pi` network looks like the Greek letter `pi`: shunt capacitor at the input, series inductor in the middle, and shunt capacitor at the output.
E7C01.explanation: The correct answer is "A capacitor is connected between the input and ground, another capacitor is connected between the output and ground, and an inductor is connected between the input and output" because that is the standard low-pass Pi arrangement.
E7C01.mnemonic: Pi low-pass is `C-L-C` with the capacitors to ground.
why_wrong: That describes an inductor-heavy network, not a low-pass Pi network.
This puts the capacitors in series instead of shunting them to ground, so it is not the correct Pi form.
That swaps inductors and capacitors into the wrong places.

E7C02.hint: A T-network with series capacitors and a shunt inductor passes high frequencies more easily than low ones, so it acts as a high-pass filter.
E7C02.explanation: The correct answer is "High-pass" because series capacitors block low frequencies while the shunt inductor helps reject them.
why_wrong: A low-pass network would more often use series inductors and shunt capacitors.
There is no tuned passband here that selects only a middle range.
A notch filter rejects a narrow band rather than passing highs.

E7C03.hint: Adding the extra `L` to make a `Pi-L` network increases filtering action, especially against unwanted harmonics. Think of it as adding another cleanup stage.
E7C03.explanation: The correct answer is "Greater harmonic suppression" because the extra inductor improves the low-pass filtering characteristics of the network.
why_wrong: Efficiency is not the main reason the extra inductor is added.
A Pi-L network does not eliminate one capacitor from the original Pi idea.
The key improvement tested here is filtering, not transformation range.

E7C04.hint: Matching networks do two jobs: tune out reactance and transform resistance. First cancel the `j` part, then make the remaining resistance look like the value you want.
E7C04.explanation: The correct answer is "It cancels the reactive part of the impedance and changes the resistive part to the desired value" because that is the basic purpose of an impedance-matching network.
E7C04.mnemonic: Match means remove reactance and reshape resistance.
why_wrong: Negative resistance is not how ordinary impedance-matching networks work.
Transconductance is not the mechanism used to cancel reactance in passive matching networks.
Reactive currents are not simply burned off in matched resistors.

E7C05.hint: Chebyshev filters trade a little ripple in the passband for a sharper cutoff than Butterworth. If you see ripple plus steep cutoff, think `Chebyshev`.
E7C05.explanation: The correct answer is "A Chebyshev filter" because Chebyshev designs are known for passband ripple and sharper transition characteristics.
E7C05.mnemonic: Chebyshev chops harder by accepting some ripple.
why_wrong: Butterworth is the flatter-response family, not the rippled sharp-cutoff one.
Active LC is not a named response family in this sense.
A passive op-amp filter is not a valid response-family answer here.

E7C06.hint: Elliptical filters are the aggressive ones: very sharp cutoff and transmission zeros, which show up as notches in the stopband.
E7C06.explanation: The correct answer is "Extremely sharp cutoff with one or more notches in the stop band" because those are the hallmark features of an elliptical filter.
why_wrong: Gradual rolloff is the opposite of the elliptical filter's defining feature.
An extremely flat passband points more toward Butterworth behavior.
Again, gradual rolloff does not describe the sharp-cutoff elliptical family.

E7C07.hint: A `Pi-L` network is exactly what its name suggests: start with a Pi network and add a series inductor on the output side.
E7C07.explanation: The correct answer is "A Pi-network with an additional output series inductor" because that is the literal meaning of Pi-L.
E7C07.mnemonic: Pi-L = Pi plus one more L.
why_wrong: Pi-L has nothing to do with 'phase inverter load.'
A Pi-L network has more than just three isolated parts in the way implied here.
The network can include grounded components, so this description is wrong.

E7C08.hint: At VHF and UHF, helical filters are common for narrow band-pass and notch work because they offer high Q in a practical package.
E7C08.explanation: The correct answer is "A helical filter" because helical resonators are commonly used as VHF/UHF band-pass and notch filters in transceivers.
why_wrong: Sallen-Key filters are active RC filters, not the usual VHF/UHF resonator answer here.
A swinging choke is a power-supply component, not a VHF/UHF selectivity filter.
FIR filters are digital-signal-processing structures, not the usual physical RF filter named here.

E7C09.hint: A crystal lattice filter uses quartz crystals as very high-Q elements for selective low-level signal filtering, especially in receivers and IF stages.
E7C09.explanation: The correct answer is "A filter for low-level signals made using quartz crystals" because crystal lattice filters are built from quartz crystals for sharp selectivity.
E7C09.mnemonic: Crystal lattice filter = crystal selectivity for small signals.
why_wrong: It is not a power-supply filter.
The definition is not limited to audio spacing between resonances.
The crystals are not physically lattice-shaped; 'lattice' describes the filter topology.

E7C10.hint: A 2-meter repeater duplexer needs high-Q physical resonators that can sharply separate transmit and receive frequencies at VHF. That is classic cavity-filter territory.
E7C10.explanation: The correct answer is "A cavity filter" because repeater duplexers commonly use cavity resonators for strong VHF isolation.
why_wrong: Crystal filters are common at IF, not as the usual duplexer elements on 2 meters.
A DSP filter cannot provide the RF duplexer isolation by itself.
Simple L-C filters usually do not provide the needed repeater duplexer performance.

E7C11.hint: Shape factor tells you how quickly a filter goes from passband to rejection. A better adjacent-channel filter has a steeper skirt, which means a better shape factor.
E7C11.explanation: The correct answer is "Shape factor" because it measures how sharply a filter transitions and therefore how well it rejects nearby channels.
E7C11.mnemonic: Shape factor describes the filter skirt shape.
why_wrong: Passband ripple is about amplitude flatness inside the passband, not adjacent-channel rejection.
Phase response concerns timing and distortion, not primarily nearby-channel selectivity.
Noise factor is a receiver noise measure, not a filter-skirt metric.

E7D01.hint: A linear regulator works by continuously adjusting how hard a pass element conducts so the output voltage stays constant as input or load changes.
E7D01.explanation: The correct answer is "The conduction of a control element is varied to maintain a constant output voltage" because that is the basic operating principle of a linear regulator.
E7D01.mnemonic: Linear regulation means smoothly varying the pass device.
why_wrong: A ramp output is not the definition of linear voltage regulation.
A pass device is still part of the regulating action.
Duty-cycle control describes switchmode regulation, not linear regulation.

E7D02.hint: A switchmode regulator chops DC into pulses and changes the duty cycle. After filtering, the average value becomes the desired output voltage.
E7D02.explanation: The correct answer is "By varying the duty cycle of pulses input to a filter" because switchmode regulators control output voltage by pulse-width control followed by filtering.
E7D02.mnemonic: Switchers set voltage by changing pulse width.
why_wrong: The goal is not alternating between positive and negative outputs.
Varying pass-element conductivity is the linear-regulator idea.
Switching between Zener references is not how ordinary switchmode regulation works.

E7D03.hint: When a circuit needs a simple stable voltage reference, the classic answer is a `Zener diode` operated in breakdown.
E7D03.explanation: The correct answer is "A Zener diode" because Zener diodes are commonly used as stable voltage references.
why_wrong: A DAC generates programmable outputs, but it is not the simple reference device being asked for.
An SCR is a switching device, not the normal precision reference here.
An ADC converts signals to numbers; it is not a reference source.

E7D04.hint: A three-terminal regulator like a `7805` is a series regulator: it sits in series with the load and controls voltage by varying the pass element.
E7D04.explanation: The correct answer is "A series regulator" because standard three-terminal regulators operate with a series pass element.
E7D04.mnemonic: Three-terminal regulators are usually series regulators.
why_wrong: It regulates voltage, not current as a source.
A shunt regulator works differently by loading the source.
A shunt current source is not the device type described here.

E7D05.hint: A shunt regulator regulates by drawing extra current from the unregulated source, effectively loading it down to hold voltage in range.
E7D05.explanation: The correct answer is "A shunt regulator" because shunt regulators operate by loading the source rather than controlling current in series with the load.
E7D05.mnemonic: Shunt regulators regulate by shunting current away.
why_wrong: A constant current source is not the same thing as a shunt regulator.
A series regulator works in series with the load, not by loading the source itself.
A shunt current source is not the intended voltage-regulator answer.

E7D06.hint: In a linear regulator, the transistor pass element adjusts current flow so the load sees a steady voltage. That is Q1's core job in the figure.
E7D06.explanation: The correct answer is "It controls the current to keep the output voltage constant" because Q1 is the regulating pass transistor in the circuit.
E7D06.mnemonic: Q1 is the pass device that controls current.
why_wrong: Negative feedback may exist in the circuit, but that is not Q1's primary labeled function here.
Q1 is not there just to be a constant load.
This is a linear regulator, not a chopping or switching regulator.

E7D07.hint: A capacitor placed around a rectifier path is usually there to smooth ripple. In this figure, `C2` bypasses ripple around `D1`.
E7D07.explanation: The correct answer is "It bypasses rectifier output ripple around D1" because C2 smooths ripple associated with the rectifier portion of the circuit.
E7D07.mnemonic: C2 is a ripple bypass cap.
why_wrong: It is not simply the main brute-force output filter capacitor.
Preventing oscillation is not the specific role identified here.
Capacitors do not provide fixed DC bias in the way described.

E7D08.hint: Because the figure uses a pass transistor regulating continuously rather than high-frequency chopping, it is a linear voltage regulator circuit.
E7D08.explanation: The correct answer is "Linear voltage regulator" because Figure E7-2 uses linear pass-element regulation rather than switching.
E7D08.mnemonic: Continuous pass control means linear regulator.
why_wrong: A switching regulator would show chopping or duty-cycle behavior instead.
It is regulating voltage, not acting as a signal amplifier stage.
A common-base amplifier is also unrelated to the regulator function shown.

E7D09.hint: Battery operating time is the simplest battery math in the pool: `time = amp-hours / average amps`.
E7D09.explanation: The correct answer is "Capacity in amp-hours divided by average current" because battery life in hours is calculated by dividing stored charge by load current.
E7D09.mnemonic: Hours from amp-hours divided by amps.
why_wrong: Current divided by capacity would invert the formula and give the wrong units.
Internal resistance is not part of the basic runtime formula here.
Again, internal resistance is not how operating time is calculated.

E7D10.hint: Switching supplies run at high frequency, and high frequency lets transformers and filter parts be much smaller for the same power. That is why they are lighter and cheaper.
E7D10.explanation: The correct answer is "The high frequency inverter design uses much smaller transformers and filter components for an equivalent power output" because component size shrinks as operating frequency rises.
why_wrong: Switching supplies still need output filtering.
Lower control-circuit current is not the main size-and-cost reason.
They are not based on recovering unused AC-cycle power in that sense.

E7D11.hint: Solar panels produce `DC`. An inverter is used when you need `AC`, so its job is DC-to-AC conversion.
E7D11.explanation: The correct answer is "Convert the panel’s output from DC to AC" because that is the fundamental purpose of an inverter in a solar power system.
why_wrong: Reducing ripple is not the primary purpose of the inverter.
Voltage regulation under varying illumination is a different function.
Preventing reverse discharge is usually done with blocking or control circuitry, not the inverter's main role.

E7D12.hint: Dropout voltage is the minimum 'headroom' a linear regulator needs between input and output before it can no longer regulate properly.
E7D12.explanation: The correct answer is "Minimum input-to-output voltage required to maintain regulation" because dropout is the minimum differential the regulator needs across itself.
why_wrong: Power-dissipation limits are different from dropout voltage.
This describes output change over input variation, not the headroom requirement.
This is also about output drop, not the regulator's required input-output differential.

E7D13.hint: A series linear regulator burns off the excess voltage as heat, so its dissipation is `Vin - Vout`, times the load current.
E7D13.explanation: The correct answer is "Voltage difference from input to output multiplied by output current" because the series pass element dissipates the dropped voltage at the output current.
E7D13.mnemonic: Linear-regulator heat = voltage drop times current.
why_wrong: Input voltage times input current gives total input power, not just regulator dissipation.
Dividing voltage by current is not the power formula.
Output voltage times output current is load power, not regulator heat.

E7D14.hint: Equal-value resistors across series filter capacitors do three useful things at once: balance voltage, discharge caps after shutdown, and provide a minimum load. When all three are true, pick `all these choices`.
E7D14.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because balancing resistors across series capacitors serve all of the listed functions.
E7D14.mnemonic: Bleeder resistors balance, bleed, and load.
why_wrong: Equalizing voltage is true, but it is only one of the reasons.
Discharging capacitors is also true, but not the only function.
Providing a minimum load can also be true, but the full answer includes all of them.

E7D15.hint: A step-start circuit limits inrush current when a high-voltage supply is first turned on. Its practical purpose is to let big filter capacitors charge gradually instead of slamming them instantly.
E7D15.explanation: The correct answer is "To allow the filter capacitors to charge gradually" because step-start circuits reduce startup surge current into the capacitors.
why_wrong: It is not for creating dual-voltage outputs.
Line-voltage compensation is a different circuit function.
Preventing switch arcing can be a side benefit, but gradual capacitor charging is the main purpose tested here.

E7E01.hint: FM phone is commonly generated by changing the frequency of an oscillator, not by modulating the final amplifier. A reactance modulator changes the effective tuning of the local oscillator.
E7E01.explanation: The correct answer is "Reactance modulation of a local oscillator" because FM can be produced by varying the oscillator frequency through reactance modulation.
E7E01.mnemonic: For FM, move the oscillator frequency.
why_wrong: Balanced modulation is associated with DSB and SSB generation, not ordinary FM phone generation.
Modulating the final amplifier is not the normal FM-generation method in this context.
Balanced modulation again points toward amplitude-based sideband generation.

E7E02.hint: A reactance modulator works by varying effective capacitance in an oscillator circuit. Changing capacitance shifts frequency or phase, which gives FM or PM.
E7E02.explanation: The correct answer is "Produce PM or FM signals by varying a capacitance" because reactance modulators simulate a changing capacitive reactance in the oscillator.
E7E02.mnemonic: Reactance modulation usually means varying effective C.
why_wrong: Varying resistance is not how reactance modulation creates FM or PM.
Inductance changes could shift tuning, but the keyed reactance-modulator answer here is varying capacitance.
AM is not the modulation type produced by this reactance-modulator function.

E7E03.hint: A frequency discriminator converts FM frequency changes back into an audio-voltage variation, so it is an FM detector.
E7E03.explanation: The correct answer is "A circuit for detecting FM signals" because a discriminator demodulates frequency-modulated signals.
why_wrong: A discriminator is not an FM generator.
Adjacent-signal filtering is a selectivity function, not discrimination of FM audio.
Automatic band switching is unrelated to FM detection.

E7E04.hint: A classic SSB method starts by making double-sideband suppressed carrier with a balanced modulator, then removing one sideband with a filter.
E7E04.explanation: The correct answer is "Use a balanced modulator followed by a filter" because that is a standard way to generate a single-sideband phone signal.
E7E04.mnemonic: Balanced modulator makes DSB, filter leaves one sideband.
why_wrong: Reactance modulation is for FM or PM, not the usual SSB-generation path.
Loop modulator followed by mixer is not the standard textbook SSB answer here.
A product detector demodulates SSB; it does not generate it.

E7E05.hint: FM systems boost higher audio frequencies before transmission to improve noise performance. That transmitter-side high-audio boost is pre-emphasis.
E7E05.explanation: The correct answer is "A pre-emphasis network" because pre-emphasis increases higher audio frequencies before FM transmission.
why_wrong: De-emphasis is the receiver-side correction, not the transmitter-side boost.
There is no standard FM block called a harmonic enhancer for this purpose.
A heterodyne enhancer is not the correct FM audio-conditioning network.

E7E06.hint: De-emphasis in the receiver complements transmitter pre-emphasis, and in amateur gear this is often tied to the way phase-modulated transmitters are made to sound like standard FM systems.
E7E06.explanation: The correct answer is "For compatibility with transmitters using phase modulation" because de-emphasis in the receiver complements the audio characteristics of PM-based FM systems.
E7E06.mnemonic: De-emphasis helps PM-based FM systems sound right.
why_wrong: Impulse-noise reduction is not the main reason named in this pool question.
Receiver de-emphasis is not about efficiency.
Third-order distortion products are not what de-emphasis is meant to remove.

E7E07.hint: Baseband is the original information signal before it gets shifted onto an RF carrier. Think audio or data before modulation.
E7E07.explanation: The correct answer is "The frequency range occupied by a message signal prior to modulation" because baseband refers to the original unmodulated information spectrum.
E7E07.mnemonic: Baseband is the message before the carrier gets involved.
why_wrong: Baseband is not the lowest tuning range of the radio.
The transmitted signal's unmodulated bandwidth is not the same definition as the original message spectrum.
It does not mean the basic FM oscillator frequency.

E7E08.hint: A mixer produces more than just one new frequency. You typically get both originals plus the sum and difference products.
E7E08.explanation: The correct answer is "The two input frequencies along with their sum and difference frequencies" because mixer nonlinearity creates those frequency components.
E7E08.mnemonic: Mixers make originals, sum, and difference.
why_wrong: Two and four times the inputs are not the principal textbook mixer outputs.
Square roots of frequency products are not mixer outputs.
Those numeric ratios are unrelated to ordinary mixer products.

E7E09.hint: If you overdrive a mixer, it creates extra unwanted combinations beyond the desired sum and difference. Those are spurious mixer products.
E7E09.explanation: The correct answer is "Spurious mixer products are generated" because excessive mixer input level increases unwanted nonlinear products.
E7E09.mnemonic: Too much mixer drive makes extra junk products.
why_wrong: Mixer blanking is not the usual named effect here.
Automatic limiting is not the main result of overdriving a mixer.
AGC voltage may change elsewhere in a receiver, but the direct mixer problem is spurious products.

E7E10.hint: A diode envelope detector strips the RF carrier down to the changing amplitude envelope by rectifying the signal and smoothing it with a filter.
E7E10.explanation: The correct answer is "By rectification and filtering of RF signals" because envelope detection works by rectifying the AM signal and filtering out the RF.
why_wrong: Zener breakdown is not how an envelope detector works.
Noise mixing in the transition region is not the normal AM detection method.
Reactance sensing is unrelated to simple diode envelope detection.

E7E11.hint: SSB has no carrier to recover audio with a simple envelope detector, so it must be mixed with a reinserted carrier in a product detector.
E7E11.explanation: The correct answer is "Product detector" because SSB demodulation requires mixing the received signal with a beat-frequency oscillator signal.
E7E11.mnemonic: Product detector multiplies SSB by a replacement carrier.
why_wrong: A discriminator is for FM detection.
A phase detector is not the standard SSB demodulator answer here.
A phase comparator is used in PLL-style circuits, not ordinary SSB demodulation.

E7F01.hint: In a direct-sampling SDR, the RF goes straight into an `ADC` instead of first being mixed down with a local oscillator. The radio becomes 'software defined' after that digitization step.
E7F01.explanation: The correct answer is "Incoming RF is digitized by an analog-to-digital converter without being mixed with a local oscillator signal" because direct sampling means the receiver digitizes RF directly.
E7F01.mnemonic: Direct sampling means RF goes direct to the ADC.
why_wrong: This describes software compilation, not radio sampling.
Digital generation of I and Q without RF amplification is not the definition being tested here.
Using a switching mixer is not direct sampling because the signal is still being mixed.

E7F02.hint: To reduce unwanted noise on SSB audio, you want a DSP filter that can adjust itself to the noise conditions. That points to an adaptive filter.
E7F02.explanation: The correct answer is "An adaptive filter" because adaptive DSP filters are used to reduce unwanted noise in received SSB signals.
E7F02.mnemonic: Adaptive filters adapt to the noise.
why_wrong: A crystal-lattice filter is a fixed analog filter, not the DSP noise-reduction answer.
A Hilbert-transform filter is used for phase-shift and SSB generation/demodulation tasks, not general SSB noise cleanup.
Phase inversion alone does not remove unwanted received noise.

E7F03.hint: Digital SSB generation often uses `I/Q` processing with a 90-degree phase relationship, and the Hilbert transform is the DSP tool that creates that phase-shift behavior.
E7F03.explanation: The correct answer is "A Hilbert-transform filter" because Hilbert-transform filters are used in DSP methods for generating SSB.
E7F03.mnemonic: Hilbert helps build the 90-degree phase shift for SSB.
why_wrong: Adaptive filters are for noise reduction, not specifically for SSB generation.
A notch filter removes a narrow unwanted tone; it does not generate SSB.
Elliptical filters are sharp analog or digital magnitude filters, not the usual SSB-generation DSP method.

E7F04.hint: One DSP method of generating SSB is to create two signal paths 90 degrees apart and combine them so one sideband cancels while the other remains. That is quadrature processing.
E7F04.explanation: The correct answer is "Signals are combined in quadrature phase relationship" because DSP SSB generation commonly relies on combining I and Q signals that are 90 degrees apart.
why_wrong: Simple mixer-product subtraction is not the described DSP SSB method.
A frequency synthesizer is not how DSP directly creates SSB in this question.
Quartz-crystal emulation is unrelated to the quadrature method here.

E7F05.hint: This is the Nyquist rule: to reproduce a signal accurately, sample at least `2x` the highest frequency component.
E7F05.explanation: The correct answer is "At least twice the rate of the highest frequency component of the signal" because accurate sampling requires meeting the Nyquist criterion.
E7F05.mnemonic: Nyquist says sample at two times the top frequency or more.
why_wrong: Half the highest frequency is far too low and causes aliasing.
Sampling at the same rate as the highest frequency is still not enough for accurate reconstruction.
Four times would work, but the minimum required answer is twice.

E7F06.hint: A `1 V` range at `1 mV` resolution needs about `1000` distinct steps. Since `2^10 = 1024`, you need `10 bits`.
E7F06.explanation: The correct answer is "10 bits" because 10 bits provide 1024 quantization levels, enough to resolve about 1000 one-millivolt steps in a 1-volt range.
why_wrong: 4 bits give only 16 levels, far too few.
6 bits give 64 levels, still far too few.
8 bits give 256 levels, not enough for 1 mV resolution over 1 volt.

E7F07.hint: An `FFT` takes a waveform described over time and shows what frequencies make it up. That means time domain to frequency domain.
E7F07.explanation: The correct answer is "Converting signals from the time domain to the frequency domain" because a Fast Fourier Transform produces a frequency-domain representation.
E7F07.mnemonic: FFT finds frequencies from time samples.
why_wrong: ADC conversion is not the function of an FFT.
DAC conversion is also not the function of an FFT.
The question asks for the forward transform direction, not the reverse.

E7F08.hint: Decimation means intentionally lowering sample rate after appropriate filtering by throwing away some samples in a controlled way.
E7F08.explanation: The correct answer is "Reducing the effective sample rate by removing samples" because that is exactly what decimation does.
why_wrong: Binary-coded decimal conversion has nothing to do with sample-rate reduction.
Decimation is not simply attenuation.
Removing significant digits changes numeric precision, not sample rate.

E7F09.hint: Before decimation, you must remove frequencies that would alias downward into false lower-frequency signals. That is the job of the anti-alias filter.
E7F09.explanation: The correct answer is "It removes high-frequency signal components that would otherwise be reproduced as lower frequency components" because aliasing folds high-frequency content into lower frequencies after sample-rate reduction.
E7F09.mnemonic: Anti-alias filter removes what would fold down.
why_wrong: It is not there to peak the response.
Low-frequency removal is the opposite of the usual anti-aliasing goal.
Notching out the sampling frequency is not the main reason for anti-alias filtering in a decimator.

E7F10.hint: In a direct-sampling SDR, maximum observable bandwidth is fundamentally tied to how fast the ADC samples. Faster sample rate means wider bandwidth.
E7F10.explanation: The correct answer is "Sample rate" because ADC sample rate determines the maximum bandwidth that can be digitized.
why_wrong: Bits determine resolution and dynamic range more than bandwidth.
Integral non-linearity affects accuracy, not maximum receive bandwidth.
Differential non-linearity also affects accuracy, not the bandwidth limit.

E7F11.hint: With no outside noise dominating, the ADC's own quantization limits matter most. Reference voltage and bit depth set how small a signal can be meaningfully resolved.
E7F11.explanation: The correct answer is "Reference voltage level and sample width in bits" because those ADC parameters determine quantization step size and thus minimum detectable level in this idealized case.
E7F11.mnemonic: ADC sensitivity depends on full-scale voltage and bit depth.
why_wrong: Clock phase noise matters for other performance limits, but not the quantization floor answer tested here.
Data storage rate is unrelated to minimum detectable signal level.
Missing codes and jitter can hurt performance, but the keyed answer here is the ADC reference level and number of bits.

E7F12.hint: A big advantage of many `FIR` filters is linear phase: all frequency components can be delayed by the same amount, which preserves waveform shape.
E7F12.explanation: The correct answer is "FIR filters can delay all frequency components of the signal by the same amount" because FIR filters can be designed for linear-phase response.
E7F12.mnemonic: FIR is famous for equal delay across frequencies.
why_wrong: FIR filters are often not easier to implement for sharp rolloff than IIR filters.
Fast impulse response is not the defining general truth here.
Since one listed statement is not generally true, 'all these choices' cannot be correct.

E7F13.hint: In DSP filters, taps represent delayed sample points that get weighted and summed. More taps means more delayed versions are available to shape the response.
E7F13.explanation: The correct answer is "Provide incremental signal delays for filter algorithms" because filter taps correspond to delayed sample contributions used in the algorithm.
why_wrong: Taps do not reduce acoustic pressure or excess signal level by definition.
They are not debugging access points.
They do not simply choose where baseband is generated.

E7F14.hint: To make a DSP filter sharper, you usually increase the number of taps. More taps give the algorithm more freedom to create steep skirts.
E7F14.explanation: The correct answer is "More taps" because increasing the tap count generally allows a sharper digital filter response.
why_wrong: Higher data rate alone does not directly create the sharper filter characteristic being asked about.
Lower Q would usually mean a broader, less selective response.
More math precision can help numerical accuracy, but it does not primarily determine filter sharpness.

E7G01.hint: An ideal op-amp behaves like a good voltage source at its output, so its output impedance is very low.
E7G01.explanation: The correct answer is "Very low" because op-amps are designed to have very low output impedance.
E7G01.mnemonic: Good amplifiers drive loads with low output impedance.
why_wrong: Very high output impedance is the opposite of the usual op-amp goal.
`100 ohms` is not the general textbook description.
`10,000 ohms` is far too high for a typical op-amp output.

E7G02.hint: Putting a capacitor across the feedback resistor reduces high-frequency gain, so the op-amp stage becomes a low-pass filter.
E7G02.explanation: The correct answer is "Low-pass filter" because the feedback capacitor increasingly shunts high frequencies, rolling off the response.
E7G02.mnemonic: Feedback capacitor cuts highs, so it becomes low-pass.
why_wrong: A high-pass response would come from attenuating lows, not highs.
There is no mid-band-only pass region here.
This does not create a narrow notch rejection.

E7G03.hint: An ideal op-amp should not load its source much, so its input impedance is very high.
E7G03.explanation: The correct answer is "Very high" because op-amps are designed to draw very little input current.
E7G03.mnemonic: Op-amp input should look almost open-circuit.
why_wrong: `100 ohms` is far too low for the typical op-amp input.
`10,000 ohms` is also much lower than the usual idealized description.
Very low input impedance would heavily load the source, which is not the op-amp ideal.

E7G04.hint: Input offset voltage is the tiny differential voltage you would need between the op-amp inputs to force the output to zero when running open loop.
E7G04.explanation: The correct answer is "The differential input voltage needed to bring the open loop output voltage to zero" because that is the definition of op-amp input offset voltage.
E7G04.mnemonic: Offset voltage is the little input difference needed to null the output.
why_wrong: That compares output and input voltages, not the nulling differential input.
This does not correctly define the nulling condition for offset voltage.
Potential between the inputs is close, but the definition specifically involves the voltage needed to make the open-loop output zero.

E7G05.hint: Too much gain and too much Q together make op-amp audio filters ring and become unstable. Keeping both under control prevents that behavior.
E7G05.explanation: The correct answer is "Restrict both gain and Q" because excessive gain and high Q both increase the tendency toward ringing and instability.
E7G05.mnemonic: High gain plus high Q equals trouble.
why_wrong: Raising Q while restricting only gain still leaves a ringing problem.
Raising gain while restricting only Q can also cause instability.
Increasing both makes the problem worse, not better.

E7G06.hint: Gain-bandwidth product is tied to the unity-gain point. It refers to the frequency where the op-amp's open-loop gain has dropped to 1.
E7G06.explanation: The correct answer is "The frequency at which the open-loop gain of the amplifier equals one" because that is the standard unity-gain interpretation of gain-bandwidth.
E7G06.mnemonic: Gain-bandwidth meets unity at the key frequency.
why_wrong: This is not just a statement about one filter's maximum usable frequency.
Filter cutoff frequency is not the definition of op-amp gain-bandwidth.
Offset voltage and gain-bandwidth are unrelated specifications.

E7G07.hint: For the Figure `E7-3` inverting op-amp, voltage gain magnitude is `RF / R1`. With `470 / 10`, the answer is `47`.
E7G07.explanation: The correct answer is "47" because the absolute voltage gain of the circuit is `470 / 10 = 47`.
E7G07.mnemonic: Inverting-op-amp gain is feedback resistor over input resistor.
why_wrong: `0.21` is the inverse ratio, not the gain magnitude.
`4700` is ten times too large.
`24` does not match the resistor ratio.

E7G08.hint: For an ideal op-amp, gain is treated as frequency-independent in the simplified model. Real op-amps roll off, but the ideal one does not.
E7G08.explanation: The correct answer is "It does not vary with frequency" because the question asks about an ideal operational amplifier.
E7G08.mnemonic: Ideal means no frequency-dependent gain loss.
why_wrong: An ideal op-amp does not gain more with frequency.
Linear decrease describes some simplified real-device behavior, not the ideal case.
Real op-amps often roll off, but the ideal model does not.

E7G09.hint: Again use the inverting-op-amp rule: gain is `-RF / R1 = -10`. Multiply `0.23 V` by `-10` to get `-2.3 V`.
E7G09.explanation: The correct answer is "-2.3 volts" because the circuit gain is `-10,000 / 1,000 = -10`, so `0.23 x -10 = -2.3` volts.
E7G09.mnemonic: Inverting gain gives the minus sign as well as the ratio.
why_wrong: `0.23 V` ignores the gain.
`2.3 V` has the right magnitude but wrong sign for an inverting stage.
`-0.23 V` ignores the gain magnitude.

E7G10.hint: Use the same op-amp gain shortcut: `68,000 / 1,800` is about `37.8`, so the nearest absolute gain is `38`.
E7G10.explanation: The correct answer is "38" because the absolute voltage gain is approximately `68k / 1.8k = 37.8`.
E7G10.mnemonic: Round the resistor ratio to the nearest listed gain.
why_wrong: `1` would mean no gain, which is not this resistor ratio.
`0.03` is the inverse-style ratio, not the gain magnitude.
`76` is roughly double the correct value.

E7G11.hint: For `RF = 47k` and `R1 = 3.3k`, the absolute gain is `47 / 3.3`, about `14.2`. Pick `14`.
E7G11.explanation: The correct answer is "14" because the circuit's absolute gain is approximately `47k / 3.3k = 14.2`.
E7G11.mnemonic: Another inverting-op-amp ratio: feedback over input.
why_wrong: `28` is about twice the correct ratio.
`7` is about half the correct ratio.
`0.07` is the inverse-style ratio instead of the gain magnitude.

E7G12.hint: An operational amplifier is the classic high-gain differential amplifier building block: direct-coupled, very high input impedance, and very low output impedance.
E7G12.explanation: The correct answer is "A high-gain, direct-coupled differential amplifier with very high input impedance and very low output impedance" because that is the standard definition of an op-amp.
E7G12.mnemonic: Op-amp = high gain, differential input, high Zin, low Zout.
why_wrong: That invents a very different kind of specialized digital-audio device.
An op-amp is not a legal-limit RF speech amplifier.
It is not specifically a UHF or microwave RF amplifier.

E7H01.hint: The three oscillator names that show up over and over in ham electronics are `Colpitts`, `Hartley`, and `Pierce`.
E7H01.explanation: The correct answer is "Colpitts, Hartley, and Pierce" because those are three classic oscillator circuit types.
why_wrong: This includes made-up or nonstandard names instead of the common oscillator trio.
These are not the standard oscillator names tested in amateur radio theory.
Again, `Taft` is not part of the classic trio.

E7H02.hint: A microphonic oscillator behaves a little like a microphone: vibration changes the circuit physically, and the frequency shifts as a result.
E7H02.explanation: The correct answer is "Changes in oscillator frequency caused by mechanical vibration" because microphonics in oscillators are frequency changes induced by vibration.
E7H02.mnemonic: Microphonic means vibration gets into the frequency.
why_wrong: This confuses the term with a microphone preamp IC.
RF on a microphone cable is a different problem from oscillator microphonics.
Microphonics are not caused by excess loading of the microphone.

E7H03.hint: A `PLL` locks a voltage-controlled oscillator to a stable reference by comparing phase, filtering the error, and steering the VCO. Think phase detector, loop filter, VCO, reference.
E7H03.explanation: The correct answer is "An electronic servo loop consisting of a phase detector, a low-pass filter, a voltage-controlled oscillator, and a stable reference oscillator" because that is the standard PLL block diagram.
E7H03.mnemonic: PLL = phase detector + loop filter + VCO + reference.
why_wrong: A ratio detector and reactance modulator are not the standard PLL blocks.
A monostable multivibrator is a one-shot, not a phase-locked loop.
A precision push-pull amplifier is unrelated to PLL operation.

E7H04.hint: A Colpitts oscillator is the one that gets feedback from a capacitive divider. If you remember `Colpitts = capacitors`, this question gets easy.
E7H04.explanation: The correct answer is "Through a capacitive divider" because a Colpitts oscillator uses a capacitive divider to provide feedback.
why_wrong: A tapped coil is the Hartley-style clue, not Colpitts.
Link coupling is not the defining Colpitts feedback method.
A neutralizing capacitor is used for stability in amplifiers, not as the main Colpitts feedback network.

E7H05.hint: A Pierce oscillator is the crystal oscillator answer. Its feedback path depends on the quartz crystal itself.
E7H05.explanation: The correct answer is "Through a quartz crystal" because the Pierce oscillator uses a quartz crystal in the feedback path.
why_wrong: A tapped coil is not the Pierce feedback method.
Link coupling is not the defining Pierce mechanism.
A neutralizing capacitor is not what makes a Pierce oscillator a Pierce oscillator.

E7H06.hint: PLLs show up in two big places in radio gear: frequency synthesis and FM demodulation. If a question asks what a PLL can do, that pair is a strong clue.
E7H06.explanation: The correct answer is "Frequency synthesis and FM demodulation" because both are standard PLL applications.
E7H06.mnemonic: PLLs synthesize frequencies and can follow FM.
why_wrong: PLL circuits are not general wideband AF/RF power amplifiers.
Photovoltaic conversion and optical coupling are unrelated technologies.
Simple digital comparison and counting does not capture the main PLL radio uses being tested.

E7H07.hint: If vibration is causing frequency wobble, the most direct cure is mechanical isolation. Keep the oscillator from being shaken by the chassis or enclosure.
E7H07.explanation: The correct answer is "Mechanically isolate the oscillator circuitry from its enclosure" because that reduces vibration-induced microphonic effects.
E7H07.mnemonic: Microphonics are mechanical, so fix them mechanically.
why_wrong: NP0 capacitors help thermal stability more than vibration isolation.
Power-supply noise reduction addresses a different problem.
Increasing gain would not reduce microphonics and could make effects more obvious.

E7H08.hint: `NP0` capacitors have extremely low temperature coefficient, which is why they are the standard choice for reducing thermal drift in crystal oscillators.
E7H08.explanation: The correct answer is "NP0 capacitors" because they offer excellent temperature stability.
E7H08.mnemonic: NP0 means nearly no temp drift.
why_wrong: Toroidal inductors are not the usual thermal-drift fix for crystal oscillators.
Wirewound resistors are not the key temperature-stable tuning component here.
Non-inductive resistors also do not address the main oscillator-drift issue.

E7H09.hint: A synthesizer using a phase accumulator, lookup table, DAC, and low-pass filter is describing `DDS`, direct digital synthesis.
E7H09.explanation: The correct answer is "A direct digital synthesizer" because those are the standard DDS building blocks.
E7H09.mnemonic: Phase accumulator plus lookup table screams DDS.
why_wrong: A hybrid synthesizer is not the specific architecture listed.
A PLL synthesizer uses different core blocks than the ones named here.
Direct conversion is a receiver architecture, not the synthesis method being described.

E7H10.hint: In a DDS, the lookup table stores the waveform samples themselves, usually amplitude values for the desired sine wave or other waveform.
E7H10.explanation: The correct answer is "Amplitude values that represent the desired waveform" because the DDS lookup table contains sample values for the synthesized waveform.
why_wrong: That is not what the waveform lookup table stores.
Again, phase relationship alone is not the stored table content being tested.
It is not a memory of favorite operating frequencies.

E7H11.hint: DDS outputs often have discrete spurs rather than just smooth broadband noise. If the question asks about major spectral impurities, think spur lines at specific frequencies.
E7H11.explanation: The correct answer is "Spurious signals at discrete frequencies" because DDS systems commonly exhibit discrete spur components.
why_wrong: Broadband noise exists, but the major named impurity is usually discrete spurs.
Digital conversion noise is too vague and not the keyed answer.
There is no local oscillator in the ordinary DDS sense being described here.

E7H12.hint: Crystals are specified for a certain load capacitance. If you want the oscillator to run on the intended frequency, you must provide that specified parallel capacitance.
E7H12.explanation: The correct answer is "Provide the crystal with a specified parallel capacitance" because crystal frequency calibration depends on the proper load capacitance.
E7H12.mnemonic: Crystal frequency depends on correct load capacitance.
why_wrong: Parallel inductance is not the standard manufacturer load specification.
Bias voltage is not what sets the specified crystal frequency in this question.
Bias current is also not the normal crystal-load specification.

E7H13.hint: For highly accurate and stable microwave references, several approaches are valid: GPS reference, rubidium standard, and temperature-controlled dielectric resonator. When all are good answers, choose `all these choices`.
E7H13.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because all three listed techniques can provide very accurate and stable oscillators.
E7H13.mnemonic: If GPS, rubidium, and controlled resonators all fit, choose all.
why_wrong: GPS reference is one correct technique, but not the only one.
Rubidium stabilization is also correct, but not uniquely so.
A temperature-controlled high-Q dielectric resonator is likewise correct, so no single one of these is complete by itself.

E8A01.hint: If a question asks what shows that a square wave is really a fundamental sine wave plus odd harmonics, that is classic Fourier analysis.
E8A01.explanation: The correct answer is "Fourier analysis" because Fourier methods decompose a waveform into sinusoidal components and their harmonics.
E8A01.mnemonic: Fourier breaks complex waves into sine waves.
why_wrong: Vector analysis is not the standard technique for harmonic decomposition of a square wave.
Numerical analysis is too broad and not the specific named method here.
Differential analysis is not the waveform-decomposition method being asked.

E8A02.hint: Of the choices listed, only `successive approximation` is a real ADC architecture. The others are unrelated circuit terms.
E8A02.explanation: The correct answer is "Successive approximation" because successive-approximation ADCs are a standard analog-to-digital conversion method.
why_wrong: Harmonic regeneration is not an ADC type.
Level shifting changes voltage range but does not convert analog to digital.
Phase reversal is not an ADC method.

E8A03.hint: A time-domain plot shows how signal amplitude changes as time passes. If time is on the horizontal axis, think amplitude at different times.
E8A03.explanation: The correct answer is "Amplitude at different times" because the time domain describes waveform amplitude versus time.
E8A03.mnemonic: Time domain means amplitude over time.
why_wrong: Power at intervals of phase is not the usual definition of a time-domain signal description.
Frequency at different times points toward spectrogram-style interpretation, not the basic time-domain description.
Discrete impulses in time order are not the general definition of time-domain representation.

E8A04.hint: Dither is intentionally added low-level noise that helps randomize quantization error so it sounds or measures less objectionable.
E8A04.explanation: The correct answer is "A small amount of noise added to the input signal to reduce quantization noise" because that is the standard meaning of dither in ADC work.
why_wrong: That describes unstable conversion behavior, not dither.
Irregular quantization step size is a different error source, not the definition of dither.
Dither is not a sample-removal method.

E8A05.hint: A true-RMS meter gives the correct effective voltage even when the waveform is not a pure sine wave. That is its main advantage over average-responding meters.
E8A05.explanation: The correct answer is "RMS is measured for both sinusoidal and non-sinusoidal signals" because a true-RMS meter properly calculates RMS for arbitrary waveforms.
E8A05.mnemonic: True RMS stays true even for ugly waveforms.
why_wrong: A true-RMS meter does not work by giving you an inverse Fourier transform to use.
It does not automatically calculate RMS noise factor as a separate feature.
Phasor conversion is not the reason to use a true-RMS meter.

E8A06.hint: An unprocessed SSB voice signal typically has a PEP-to-average ratio of about `2.5:1`. It is not anywhere near `25:1`, and it is definitely not `1:1` like a constant-envelope mode.
E8A06.explanation: The correct answer is "2.5 to 1" because that is the approximate crest factor for an unprocessed SSB phone signal.
E8A06.mnemonic: SSB voice is about two-and-a-half to one.
why_wrong: `25:1` is far too large for normal unprocessed SSB speech.
`1:1` would imply no voice peak variation at all.
`13:1` is also much too large for the usual value.

E8A07.hint: The PEP-to-average ratio of ordinary SSB mainly depends on how the person talks: pauses, consonants, emphasis, and overall speech dynamics.
E8A07.explanation: The correct answer is "Speech characteristics" because the crest factor of an unprocessed SSB signal is primarily determined by the nature of the voice waveform.
E8A07.mnemonic: Voice peaks depend on the voice itself.
why_wrong: The audio frequency alone does not determine the PEP-to-average ratio.
Carrier suppression is not the main factor for an unprocessed SSB voice crest factor.
Amplifier gain changes level, not the inherent speech peak-to-average relationship.

E8A08.hint: Flash or direct-conversion ADCs are used in SDRs because they are fast enough to digitize very high-frequency signals directly.
E8A08.explanation: The correct answer is "Very high speed allows digitizing high frequencies" because high sample speed is the key reason flash converters are useful in SDR front ends.
E8A08.mnemonic: Flash ADCs are chosen for speed.
why_wrong: Very low power consumption is not the main reason they are chosen here.
Out-of-sequence coding immunity is not the keyed reason in this question.
Since not all listed claims are correct, `all these choices` is wrong.

E8A09.hint: An `N`-bit ADC can encode `2^N` levels. For `8 bits`, that is `2^8 = 256`.
E8A09.explanation: The correct answer is "256" because an 8-bit converter has 256 possible quantization levels.
why_wrong: `8` is the number of bits, not the number of input levels.
Amplifier gain does not change the number of coded states in an 8-bit converter.
Again, input amplifier gain does not change the 256 available digital levels.

E8A10.hint: A DAC output contains staircase steps and sampling images. The low-pass filter after the DAC smooths those out and removes spurious sampling artifacts.
E8A10.explanation: The correct answer is "Remove spurious sampling artifacts from the output signal" because the DAC output filter suppresses unwanted image and stair-step components.
E8A10.mnemonic: DAC low-pass filter cleans up the stair-step output.
why_wrong: The DAC output filter is not there to lower input bandwidth.
It does not fix out-of-sequence codes at the input.
Because the other listed purposes are not correct, `all these choices` is wrong.

E8A11.hint: For ADC quality, one common metric is how much distortion it adds. `THD`, total harmonic distortion, is a standard converter-quality measure.
E8A11.explanation: The correct answer is "Total harmonic distortion" because THD is a recognized measure of ADC signal quality.
E8A11.mnemonic: THD is a quality metric for converters and amplifiers.
why_wrong: Peak envelope power is a transmitter power measure, not an ADC quality metric.
Reciprocal mixing is a receiver dynamic-range issue, not the ADC quality measure asked here.
Power factor is an AC power-system term, not a converter-quality spec.

E8B01.hint: FM modulation index is the simplest FM ratio in the pool: deviation divided by modulating frequency.
E8B01.explanation: The correct answer is "The ratio of frequency deviation to modulating signal frequency" because that is the definition of FM modulation index.
E8B01.mnemonic: Modulation index = deviation over modulating frequency.
why_wrong: Signal amplitude is not part of the definition of FM modulation index.
Transmitted bandwidth is not in the basic ratio definition.
This reverses the wrong quantities into the wrong ratio.

E8B02.hint: For phase modulation, the modulation index is tied to phase deviation, not to what RF carrier frequency you happen to put it on.
E8B02.explanation: The correct answer is "It does not depend on the RF carrier frequency" because phase-modulation index is independent of the actual RF carrier frequency.
E8B02.mnemonic: PM index does not care what carrier frequency you use.
why_wrong: It does not grow with RF carrier frequency.
It also does not shrink with carrier frequency.
There is no square-root carrier-frequency dependence here.

E8B03.hint: Use `index = deviation / highest modulating frequency`. Here that is `3000 / 1000 = 3`.
E8B03.explanation: The correct answer is "3" because the modulation index is `3000 Hz / 1000 Hz = 3`.
why_wrong: `0.3` is off by a factor of 10.
`6` would double the correct ratio.
`0.6` is also the wrong ratio.

E8B04.hint: Same formula again: `6000 / 2000 = 3`. When the deviation is three times the highest modulating frequency, the index is `3`.
E8B04.explanation: The correct answer is "3" because the modulation index is `6 kHz / 2 kHz = 3`.
why_wrong: `0.3` is much too small.
`0.6` is still too small.
`6` is double the correct value.

E8B05.hint: Deviation ratio uses the same style of division: `5000 / 3000`, which is about `1.67`.
E8B05.explanation: The correct answer is "1.67" because the deviation ratio is `5 kHz / 3 kHz = 1.67`.
why_wrong: `6` is far too large.
`0.167` is off by a factor of 10.
`0.6` is also too small.

E8B06.hint: For `7.5 kHz` deviation and `3.5 kHz` highest audio, divide `7.5 / 3.5` to get about `2.14`.
E8B06.explanation: The correct answer is "2.14" because the deviation ratio is `7.5 / 3.5 = 2.14` approximately.
why_wrong: `0.214` is off by a decimal place.
`0.47` is the inverse-style mistake.
`47` is wildly too large.

E8B07.hint: `OFDM` is a digital communication technique, so if the question asks what kind of amateur communication uses it, think digital modes.
E8B07.explanation: The correct answer is "Digital modes" because OFDM is a digital modulation and multiplexing technique.
E8B07.mnemonic: OFDM lives in the digital world.
why_wrong: Extremely low-power contacts like WSPR are not the general category being asked here.
EME is a path or operating style, not the modulation category.
OFDM is allowed where otherwise permitted; this statement is false.

E8B08.hint: OFDM splits data across many carefully spaced subcarriers so they stay orthogonal and avoid intersymbol interference. That is the core idea to remember.
E8B08.explanation: The correct answer is "A digital modulation technique using subcarriers at frequencies chosen to avoid intersymbol interference" because that is the defining description of OFDM.
why_wrong: This is not ordinary FM with unrelated frequencies.
Fourier transforms are used in implementations, but OFDM is not best defined here as bandwidth compression.
OFDM is not specifically a narrow-band slow-speed mode.

E8B09.hint: Deviation ratio is just another name for the ratio of maximum frequency deviation to highest audio modulating frequency.
E8B09.explanation: The correct answer is "The ratio of the maximum carrier frequency deviation to the highest audio modulating frequency" because that is the definition of deviation ratio.
E8B09.mnemonic: Deviation ratio = deviation over top audio frequency.
why_wrong: Carrier center frequency is not part of the deviation-ratio definition.
Again, the center carrier frequency is not the correct denominator.
Average audio frequency is not the specified reference value.

E8B10.hint: Frequency division multiplexing means multiple information streams occupy different frequency slices at the same time.
E8B10.explanation: The correct answer is "Dividing the transmitted signal into separate frequency bands that each carry a different data stream" because that is the definition of FDM.
why_wrong: Jumping from band to band sounds more like hopping, not multiplexing.
Packetizing data is not the same thing as FDM.
Pulse-position modulation is unrelated to the definition of FDM.

E8B11.hint: Time division multiplexing shares one channel by giving each signal its own repeating time slot.
E8B11.explanation: The correct answer is "Two or more signals are arranged to share discrete time slots of a data transmission" because that is the definition of digital TDM.
why_wrong: Sub-carriers point to frequency division, not time division.
Using time of transmission as a subcarrier is not the standard TDM definition.
Quadrature modulation is a different concept from time-slot sharing.

E8C01.hint: `QAM` uses two same-frequency carriers in quadrature, meaning 90 degrees apart, and varies their amplitudes to carry data.
E8C01.explanation: The correct answer is "Transmission of data by modulating the amplitude of two carriers of the same frequency but 90 degrees out of phase" because that is the defining idea behind quadrature amplitude modulation.
E8C01.mnemonic: QAM = quadrature carriers plus amplitude changes.
why_wrong: That describes a compression scheme, not QAM.
That describes a phase-shift SSB idea, not quadrature amplitude modulation.
Analog TV modulation is not the definition of QAM here.

E8C02.hint: Symbol rate is about how often the signal changes state to send information, not how many human-readable characters are sent.
E8C02.explanation: The correct answer is "The rate at which the waveform changes to convey information" because symbols are the distinct transmitted states or waveform changes.
why_wrong: Control-character count is unrelated to symbol rate.
FEC correction rate is not the definition of symbol rate.
Characters per second can differ from symbol rate depending on the coding scheme.
